---
title: "Supplementary Information"
subtitle: "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity"
shorttitle: "Bayesian models of thermal tolerance — Supplementary information"
authors:
  - name: "Daniel W.A. Noble"
    email: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Pieter A. Arnold"
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Shinichi Nakagawa"
    affiliations:
      - ref: ualberta
  - name: "Patrice Pottier"
    email: patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: gu
affiliations:
  - id: anu
    name: Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University
    city: Canberra
    country: Australia
  - id: gu
    name: Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg
    city: Gothenburg
    country: Sweden
  - id: ualberta
    name: Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta
    city: Edmonton
    state: Alberta
    country: Canada
date: today
output-file: supp
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    code-tools: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    format-links: false
  docx:
    reference-doc: ../bib/templates/template.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
    top-level-division: section
    keep-tex: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    colorlinks: true
    link-citations: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % tinytable LaTeX dependencies. tinytable normally injects these
        % via knitr::knit_meta_add() when its tt() chunks execute, but
        % cache-hit renders skip that injection and the build fails with
        % "Environment tblr undefined". Including the (idempotent)
        % packages here makes the preamble correct in either case.
        % `\UseTblrLibrary{siunitx}` is intentionally omitted — including
        % it manually conflicts with tinytable's auto-inject when chunks
        % run fresh, and our tables don't use S-aligned columns.
        \usepackage{tabularray}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayUnderline}[1]{\underline{#1}}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayStrikeout}[1]{\sout{#1}}
        % Author block: Quarto's default \author{names \and …} gives
        % names only. We override with authblk to pin affiliations from
        % YAML to the title page.
        \usepackage[affil-it]{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authsep{, }
        \renewcommand\Authand{, }
        \renewcommand\Authands{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\itshape\small}
        \let\OldAuthor\author
        \renewcommand{\author}[1]{}
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Daniel W.A. Noble\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{daniel.noble@anu.edu.au}}}%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Pieter A. Arnold}%
          \OldAuthor[2]{Shinichi Nakagawa}%
          \OldAuthor[3]{Patrice Pottier\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se}}}%
          \affil[1]{Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia}%
          \affil[2]{Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada}%
          \affil[3]{Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden}%
        }
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Daniel W.A. Noble**^1,\*^, **Pieter A. Arnold**^1^, **Shinichi Nakagawa**^2^, **Patrice Pottier**^3,\*^

^1^Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia.
^2^Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada.
^3^Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden.
^\*^Corresponding authors: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au; patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
:::

```{r setup}
#| label: setup-supp
#| cache: false
#| include: false
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

# Load packages, register render dependencies and set shared paths.
# Keep setup uncached so package loading always runs.

# Optional development reinstall:
# remotes::install_github("daniel1noble/bayesTLS", force = TRUE)

# Load required packages.
pacman::p_load(here, dplyr, tidyr, tibble, purrr, ggplot2, brms, posterior, tidybayes, tinytable, patchwork, pander, readxl, bayesTLS)

# Register tinytable PDF dependencies even when table chunks are cached.
invisible(tinytable::tt(data.frame(x = 1)))

# Track upstream chunk dependencies for the global cache.
knitr::opts_chunk$set(autodep = TRUE)
knitr::dep_auto()

set.seed(123)

# Directory for cached brms fits.
models_dir <- here::here("output", "models")
if (!dir.exists(models_dir)) dir.create(models_dir, recursive = TRUE)
```

# **Introduction**

This supplement demonstrates the joint Bayesian 4-parameter logistic (4PL) framework from the main manuscript. To cite the approach and the associated R package please use:

Noble, D.W.A., Arnold, P.A., Nakagawa, S. & Pottier, P. (in preparation). A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity.

The supplement is broken into two parts. The first, introducing readers to the `bayesTLS` R package and showing how it can be used. We apply the package to simulated data and also a series of case studies. The second part, shows extended simulation results associated with the main manuscript and provides the code to reproduce the figures in the main manuscript. 

# **Introduction to `bayesTLS` R Package**

We make use of the **`bayesTLS`** R package included with this project, and show how it can be used. The package fits the 4PL, extracts classical TLS quantities ($z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$) with posterior uncertainty. It can also fit, direct models for $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, which means one can model differences in groups or estimate random effects for $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ directly, making interpretation and inference quite easy. **`bayesTLS`** also uses these models to predict heat-injury accumulation and survival under arbitrary temperature time series, optionally incorporating a Sharpe-Schoolfield repair kernel [@arnoldi2025]. It also contains all the data used throughout the manuscript and supplement.

The **`bayesTLS`** package is also complimented by the **`freqTLS`** package, which uses frequentist approaches to fit the same models. It has advantages of being faster which can help with large datasets, but generally yields similar results without the need for priors. For the purpose of this tutorial, we demonstrate **`bayesTLS`**.

To access the `bayesTLS` R package, install it once from GitHub. Installing
`bayesTLS` pulls in its modelling dependencies (`brms`, `dplyr`, `ggplot2`,
`posterior`, `tibble`, `patchwork`) automatically; the tutorial also uses a few
small helper packages:

```{r install-bayesTLS}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show
# Install bayesTLS from GitHub.
# install.packages("remotes")   # if needed
remotes::install_github("daniel1noble/bayesTLS")

# Install helper packages used in the tutorial.
install.packages(c("here", "purrr", "tidyr"))
```

Then load the package and helpers, and choose a folder in which to cache the
fitted models. Every fit below passes `file = file.path(models_dir, "...")`, so
re-running a chunk reloads the saved model instead of refitting it. Run this
setup chunk once, before the analyses that follow:

```{r load-bayesTLS}
#| label: load-bayesTLS
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Load bayesTLS and helper packages.
# Install pacman first if needed.
if (!requireNamespace("pacman", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(bayesTLS, brms, dplyr, tidyr, purrr, ggplot2, here)

set.seed(123)

# Cache fitted brms models in output/models.
models_dir <- here::here("output", "models")
if (!dir.exists(models_dir)) dir.create(models_dir, recursive = TRUE)
```


The functions in `bayesTLS` chain together as a single pipeline with the following logic:

1. **Standardise** raw experimental data to a common column schema (`temp`, `duration`, `n_total`, `n_surv`, …).
2. **Fit** the joint Bayesian 4PL with the built-in `beta_binomial(link = "identity")` family and asymptote-bounded reparameterisation.
3. **Extract** $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$, all with posterior uncertainty.
4. **Predict** survival and heat-injury accumulation under fluctuating field temperature traces, with optional repair.
5. **Plot** each step with shared theming so figures across the workflow read consistently.

Every exported function is documented (`?fit_4pl`, etc.) and tested against known simulation truths. The supplement first demonstrates the pipeline in controlled simulations and then applies it to brown shrimp, zebrafish, and snow gum leaf PSII and *Drosophila* datasets.

## *Description of Functions* {#sec-function-tour}

#### *Core functions*

@tbl-function-core lists the key functions that are part of the `bayesTLS` package. Generally, these are the ones users will use most. These functions are used for standardising data, modelling, deriving the TLS quantities of interest, and predicting heat injury and survival.

In addition, if `brms` 4PL models are fit manually to data we also provide a `tls()` function that works more generally across fitted models to derive relevant TLS quantities.

```{r function-core-table}
#| label: tbl-function-core
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Core `bayesTLS` functions for a standard workflow, following the five pipeline steps. `tls()` is the general entry point for deriving TDT quantities from any fitted 4PL, including hand-coded `brms` models. See @tbl-function-tour for the full list of exported functions."

# List the core bayesTLS functions used in the main workflow.
tdt_function_core <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Step,          ~Function,                     ~Purpose,
  "Standardise",  "standardize_data()",          "Reshape raw experimental data to the package schema (temp, duration, counts).",
  "Fit",          "fit_4pl()",                   "Fit the joint Bayesian 4PL in one call (builds priors and the brms formula internally). Compare moderator groups in one joint fit with `by = \"<moderator>\"` (midpoint) or `ctmax = ~ 0 + <moderator>` (direct CTmax/z).",
  "Extract",      "extract_tdt()",               "Derive z, CTmax and (for lethal endpoints) T_crit from a `fit_4pl()` model, with full posterior uncertainty; per-group results for fits whose CTmax/z vary by a moderator (`by =`).",
  "Extract",      "tls()",                       "Derive the same quantities per moderator group from ANY fitted 4PL, including hand-coded `brms` models (`by =`); `target_surv` picks the relative or absolute threshold.",
  "Predict",      "predict_survival_curves()",   "Posterior survival across a temperature x duration grid, with credible bands (per group via `by =`).",
  "Predict",      "predict_heat_injury()",       "Heat-injury accumulation and predicted survival under a fluctuating temperature trace (optional repair; per group via `by =`).",
  "Plot",         "plot_tdt_curve()",            "Visualise the TDT (LT_x) curve on a log-time axis; companion `plot_*` functions cover each workflow step."
)

tinytable::tt(tdt_function_core, escape = TRUE)
```

#### *Full package functions*
Theer are many helper functions in the package that maybe useful for various users. @tbl-function-tour lists every exported function in the library, grouped by pipeline stage with a description of what they are designed to do.

```{r function-tour-table}
#| label: tbl-function-tour
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Functions exported by the TDT analysis library, grouped by pipeline stage. Each function is documented with roxygen2 in its R file; runnable examples accompany every function."

# List all exported bayesTLS functions by workflow stage.
tdt_function_tour <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Stage,             ~Function,                            ~Purpose,
  "Data",             "standardize_data()",                 "Rename raw columns to project schema; attach metadata.",
  "Model spec",       "make_4pl_priors()",                  "Weakly informative priors for the disjoint-bounds 4PL.",
  "Model spec",       "make_4pl_formula()",                 "brms non-linear formula; identity link, all four sub-parameters get temp_c. Midpoint or direct CTmax/z parameterisation; `by` fits `~ temp_c * moderator` on all four sub-parameters, `mid` sets the midpoint sub-model explicitly.",
  "Fit",              "fit_4pl()",                          "Joint Bayesian 4PL fit; returns a `bayes_tls` object (use `print()`, `summary()`, `plot()`). Group by a moderator with `by = \"<moderator>\"` (midpoint) or `ctmax = ~ 0 + <moderator>` (direct); the moderator is recorded so downstream readers auto-group.",
  "Inspection",       "print.bayes_tls()",                  "Compact one-screen header: data shape, T_bar, asymptote bounds, random-effect grouping, fit status (draws if fitted, spec only otherwise).",
  "Inspection",       "summary.bayes_tls()",                "Delegates to `summary()` on the underlying `brmsfit`; returns a `summary.brmsfit` with the population-level coefficient table, group-level SDs, family parameters, and HMC diagnostics laid out by brms. Extra arguments (e.g. `prob`, `robust`) are passed through.",
  "Inspection",       "plot.bayes_tls()",                   "Delegates to `plot()` on the underlying `brmsfit`; returns brms's default `mcmc_combo` (density + post-warmup trace, chains coloured) for inspecting MCMC mixing. Extra arguments (e.g. `pars`, `combo`) are passed through.",
  "Inspection",       "has_fit()",                          "Predicate: is a `bayes_tls` workflow fitted (TRUE) or spec-only (FALSE)?",
  "Inspection",       "extract_4pl_pars()",                 "Posterior draws of the four natural-scale 4PL parameters (low, up, k, mid) at the centring temperature T_bar (per group via `by =`).",
  "Inspection",       "get_brmsfit()",                      "Return the underlying `brmsfit` object held inside a `bayes_tls` workflow (for bespoke brms post-processing).",
  "Inspection",       "print.tls()",                        "Compact print method for a `tls` object: shows the per-group TDT summary table.",
  "Accessors",        "get_z_draws()",                      "Per-draw posterior of z from an `extract_tdt()` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_ctmax_draws()",                  "Per-draw posterior of CTmax_1hr from an `extract_tdt()` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_tcrit_draws()",                  "Per-draw posterior of T_crit from an `extract_tdt(lethal = TRUE)` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_z_summary()",                    "Posterior summary (median + 95% CrI) of z from an `extract_tdt()` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_ctmax_summary()",                "Posterior summary (median + 95% CrI) of CTmax from an `extract_tdt()` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_tcrit_summary()",                "Posterior summary (median + 95% CrI) of T_crit from an `extract_tdt(lethal = TRUE)` output.",
  "Accessors",        "get_surv_draws()",                   "Per-draw posterior of survival probability from `predict_survival_curves()`.",
  "Accessors",        "get_hi_draws()",                     "Per-draw posterior of heat-injury integrals from `predict_heat_injury()`.",
  "Diagnostics",      "diagnose_tdt_fit()",                 "Rhat / ESS / divergences / treedepth, with pass-fail flags.",
  "Diagnostics",      "tdt_parameter_table()",              "Posterior summary on the natural scale (low, up, k, mid, z; or low, up, k, CTmax, z for a direct fit), per group via `by =`.",
  "Predictions",      "predict_survival_curves()",          "Survival curves on a temp x duration grid with credible bands (per group via `by =`).",
  "Predictions",      "derive_tdt_landscape()",             "Dense 2-D survival surface for a heatmap.",
  "Predictions",      "summarise_observed_survival()",      "Observed survival mean and SE per (temp, duration) cell; an overlay for the plotted posterior curves.",
  "TDT quantities",   "derive_tdt_curve()",                 "Time to the threshold survival across temperature. Default `target_surv = \"absolute\"` (the 50% LT50); pass `\"relative\"` (`(low + up)/2` per draw) or a numeric for another probability.",
  "TDT quantities",   "derive_temperature_for_duration()",  "Temperature at threshold survival for a fixed exposure duration (same `target_surv` modes; per group via `by =`).",
  "TDT quantities",   "derive_z()",                         "Thermal sensitivity z read directly from the posterior (relative: -1/slope of mid(T); absolute: pooled per-draw local z over assay temps); optional local z(T); per group via `by =`.",
  "TDT quantities",   "extract_tdt()",                      "Bundled wrapper: z and CTmax always; T_crit opt-in via `lethal = TRUE` (rate-multiplier; lethal-endpoint data only). Same `target_surv` argument as the primitives above; per-group output for grouped fits (`by =`).",
  "TDT quantities",   "tls()",                              "One call returning z, CTmax and (when `lethal = TRUE`) T_crit per moderator group from ANY fitted 4PL, including hand-coded `brms` models. Evaluates each sub-parameter at a moderator x temperature grid via `posterior_linpred()`; `target_surv` selects the relative midpoint or absolute LT50 threshold.",
  "TDT quantities",   "tls_z()",                            "Convenience wrapper for `tls(params = \"z\")` — z only.",
  "TDT quantities",   "tls_ctmax()",                        "Convenience wrapper for `tls(params = \"ctmax\")` — CTmax only.",
  "TDT quantities",   "tls_tcrit()",                        "Convenience wrapper for `tls(params = \"tcrit\", lethal = TRUE)` — T_crit only.",
  "Two-stage",        "ts_stage1()",                        "Classical Stage 1: per-temperature dose-response (binomial `stats::glm` or beta-binomial `glmmTMB`), reading off log10(LT50) with validity flags.",
  "Two-stage",        "ts_stage2()",                        "Classical Stage 2: OLS of log10(LT50) on temperature, deriving z, CTmax and T_crit. The canonical two-stage implementation shared by the tutorial, case studies and the bias simulation.",
  "Two-stage",        "ts_ci()",                            "Two-stage uncertainty: delta-method Normal/t intervals (method = delta) or slope-CI + MVN propagation with a line band (method = mvn).",
  "Two-stage",        "ts_curve()",                         "Median LT-vs-temperature line from a two-stage fit.",
  "Heat injury",      "make_temperature_scenarios()",       "Three reference traces (flat / single spike / multi-spike).",
  "Heat injury",      "planted_dose_from_trace()",          "Analytical HI under known z, CTmax, T_c (validation truth).",
  "Heat injury",      "predict_heat_injury()",              "Posterior HI and survival under a temperature trace (per group via `by =`).",
  "Heat injury",      "repair_rate_schoolfield()",          "Sharpe-Schoolfield repair TPC (Kelvin internally).",
  "Plotting",         "plot_survival_curves()",             "Posterior curves with observed proportion overlay.",
  "Plotting",         "plot_tdt_curve()",                   "LT_x curve on a log-time axis (classical TDT view).",
  "Plotting",         "plot_tdt_landscape()",               "Survival heatmap with contour overlay.",
  "Plotting",         "plot_temperature_density()",         "Posterior density for CTmax / T_crit with a 95-percent CrI bar.",
  "Plotting",         "plot_temperature_scenarios()",       "Three traces stacked vertically.",
  "Plotting",         "plot_heat_injury()",                 "HI and predicted survival trajectories with CrI bands.",
  "Plotting",         "plot_repair_rate()",                 "Sharpe-Schoolfield TPC curve.",
  "Plotting",         "theme_tdt()",                        "Shared project ggplot theme (minimal classic, bold axis titles) used by all package plots.",
  "Utilities",        "tdt_quantile()",                     "Quantile wrapper with TDT-friendly defaults (2.5 / 50 / 97.5%).",
  "Utilities",        "format_interval()",                  "Format a median with its bounds as a `median [lower, upper]` string.",
  "Utilities",        "clock_to_minutes()",                 "Convert clock/time formats (HH:MM:SS, hms, POSIXt, Excel time fractions, bare numerics) to minutes."
)

tinytable::tt(tdt_function_tour, escape = TRUE)
```

# **A Tutorial with Simulations**

This tutorial uses simulated data to show how the Bayesian hierarchical 4PL recovers thermal sensitivity ($z$) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ in both binomial and beta-binomial (overdispersed) settings. The joint model propagates uncertainty through downstream quantities and accommodates extensions that are awkward in a two-stage analysis. The subsequent case studies apply the same workflow to data presented in the main manuscript.

### *Simulating Data*
#### Step 1 — Setting up parameters {#sec-sim-step1}

We choose values that might be typical of a thermal mortality assay. The four parameters are the lower and upper asymptotes ($\ell, \text{up}$), the slope on the $\log_{10}$-time axis ($k$), and a midpoint that varies linearly with temperature: $\text{mid}(T) = \beta_0 + \beta_1(T - \bar T)$. Each value has a clear biological meaning (detailed in the code below).

```{r sim-params}
#| label: sim-params
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Define the generating parameters for the tutorial simulation.
true_params <- list(
  ell      = 0.03,   # 3% of individuals survive even at the longest exposures
  up       = 0.97,   # 97% are alive at very short exposures
  k        = 8,      # steepness of the survival drop on the log10(t) axis
  m_beta0  = 1.5,    # midpoint at the grand-mean temperature: ~31.6 min
  m_beta1  = -0.15,  # midpoint drops 0.15 log10-min per °C; z = -1/beta1 ≈ 6.7 °C
  T_bar    = 34      # grand-mean temperature
)
```

The implied thermal sensitivity is $z = -1/\beta_1 \approx 6.7$ °C: a temperature change of this magnitude shifts LT50 by a factor of 10. The same parameters determine the uncentred TDT intercept $\alpha = \beta_0 + \tfrac{1}{k}\log\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \ell} - \beta_1\bar{T}$ and the 1-hour critical thermal limit $CT_{max_{1hr}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \alpha)/\beta_1$. We use these known values to assess how well the models are performing, but do a proper simulation in @sec-extendsims.

We first calculate $\alpha$, $z$, and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from the generating parameters to provide reference values for the fitted models.

```{r sim-truth}
#| label: sim-truth
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Compute the true TDT summaries implied by the simulation parameters.
true_z      <- -1 / true_params$m_beta1
# Convert the centred midpoint model to the original temperature scale.
true_alpha  <- true_params$m_beta0 +
  (1 / true_params$k) *
  log((true_params$up - 0.5) / (0.5 - true_params$ell)) -
  true_params$m_beta1 * true_params$T_bar
true_CTmax  <- (log10(60) - true_alpha) / true_params$m_beta1

# Tcrit uses the geometric midpoint of the default heat-injury rate range.
true_T_crit <- true_CTmax - 2.5 * true_z
```

#### Step 2 — Simulated study design {#sec-sim-step2}

We mimic a well-replicated TDT experiment: five assay temperatures crossed with six log-spaced exposure durations, with 30 replicates of 30 individuals per cell. The resulting 900 trials and 27,000 individuals make parameter recovery clear but the the case studies below and simulations illustrate less information-rich designs.

```{r sim-design}
#| label: sim-design
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Build the temperature-by-duration sampling design for the simulation.
temps     <- c(30, 32, 34, 36, 38)             # 5 assay temperatures (°C)
durations <- c(1, 5, 15, 45, 135, 405)         # 6 durations (minutes, log-spaced)
n_rep     <- 30                                # 30 replicates per cell
n_per_rep <- 30                                # 30 individuals per replicate

design <- tidyr::expand_grid(
  T   = temps,
  t   = durations,
  rep = seq_len(n_rep)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log10_t = log10(t),
    T_c     = T - true_params$T_bar,                        # mean-centred T
    mid     = true_params$m_beta0 + true_params$m_beta1 * T_c,  # mid(T)
    p_true  = true_params$ell +
      (true_params$up - true_params$ell) /
      (1 + exp(true_params$k * (log10_t - mid))),
    n = n_per_rep
  )
```

#### Step 3 — Look at the truth before simulating {#sec-sim-step3}

Before generating noisy data, let's plot the *true* survival surface (@fig-true-surface) so we know what the model is being asked to recover. Each line is the 4PL at one assay temperature, plotted against $\log_{10}$ exposure time.

::: {#fig-true-surface}

```{r true-surface}
#| label: plot-true-surface
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4

# Plot the true survival surface before sampling noise is added.
truth_grid <- tidyr::expand_grid(
  T = temps,
  t = 10 ^ seq(log10(0.5), log10(1000), length.out = 200)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log10_t = log10(t),
    T_c     = T - true_params$T_bar,
    mid     = true_params$m_beta0 + true_params$m_beta1 * T_c,
    p_true  = true_params$ell +
      (true_params$up - true_params$ell) /
      (1 + exp(true_params$k * (log10_t - mid)))
  )

ggplot2::ggplot(truth_grid,
                ggplot2::aes(log10_t, p_true, colour = factor(T))) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  ggplot2::geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = expression(log[10]~exposure~time~(min)),
    y = "True survival probability",
    colour = "Temperature (°C)"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()
```

The known 4PL dose-response surface used to generate the simulated data, evaluated on a dense time grid at each of the five assay temperatures. The horizontal line marks 50% survival; where each curve crosses it gives the true LT50 at that temperature.
:::

Notice the curves shift left as temperature rises: hotter temperatures kill faster, so the LT50 is reached at shorter durations.

#### Step 4 — Generate noisy data: binomial and beta-binomial {#sec-sim-step4}

Survival counts may exhibit two forms of variation. Under **binomial** sampling, each replicate's survival count is drawn from $\text{Binomial}(n, p)$, where $p$ is given by the 4PL surface. **Beta-binomial** sampling adds *overdispersion*: replicate-to-replicate variation in $p$, for example from cohort effects or unmodelled heterogeneity. When TDT data show this extra variation, the beta-binomial likelihood is more appropriate.

```{r sim-data}
#| label: sim-data
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Simulate binomial and beta-binomial counts from the true 4PL surface.
# (a) Binomial: y ~ Binomial(n, p_true)
sim_bin <- design |>
  dplyr::mutate(y = rbinom(dplyr::n(), size = n, prob = p_true))

# (b) Beta-binomial: replicate-level p drawn from Beta(p*phi, (1-p)*phi)
phi <- 5  # smaller phi = more overdispersion
sim_bb <- design |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    p_draw = rbeta(dplyr::n(), p_true * phi, (1 - p_true) * phi),
    y      = rbinom(dplyr::n(), size = n, prob = p_draw)
  )
```

### *The classical two-stage TDT pipeline* {#sec-twostage}

We first run the simulated data through the **classical two-stage TDT pipeline** [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022], which is dominant in the thermal-tolerance literature.

1. **Stage 1.** At each assay temperature, fit a logistic dose-response curve to the count data and read off a point estimate of $\log_{10}\text{LT50}_T$.
2. **Stage 2.** Regress those Stage-1 point estimates on assay temperature with ordinary least squares, then derive $z = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \alpha)/\beta_1$ from the fitted line.

#### Stage 1 — Dose-response curves at each temperature {#sec-twostage-step1}

We fit a binomial GLM with a logit link separately at each assay temperature. This is a common Stage-1 specification in the TDT literature: a two-parameter logistic with asymptotes constrained to 0% and 100% [e.g., @orsted_suzukii_2024]. The midpoint $\log_{10}\text{LT50}_T = -\beta_0/\beta_1$ falls out of the GLM's two coefficients.

```{r stage1-twostage}
#| label: stage1-twostage
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Fit the per-temperature dose-response curves for the classical Stage-1 analysis.
# Stage 1 uses the package function ts_stage1(): a per-temperature binomial GLM
# (the field-default Stage 1), applied here to both the binomial and the
# overdispersed beta-binomial datasets.
stage1_bin <- ts_stage1(sim_bin, temp = "T", duration = "t",
                        n_surv = "y", n_total = "n", family = "binomial")
stage1_bb  <- ts_stage1(sim_bb,  temp = "T", duration = "t",
                        n_surv = "y", n_total = "n", family = "binomial")
```

```{r stage1-lt50-table}
#| label: tbl-stage1-lt50
#| tbl-cap: "Per-temperature Stage-1 estimates of $\\log_{10}\\text{LT50}$ from a binomial GLM with logit link, fit separately at each of the five assay temperatures. Truth values are the simulated midpoints (which coincide with $\\log_{10}\\text{LT50}$ here because the simulation asymptotes are near 0 and 1)."

# Format the Stage-1 LT50 estimates and simulation truth.
stage1_table <- dplyr::full_join(
  stage1_bin |> dplyr::select(temp, bin_log10_lt50 = log10_lt50),
  stage1_bb  |> dplyr::select(temp, bb_log10_lt50  = log10_lt50),
  by = "temp"
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    truth_log10_lt50 = true_params$m_beta0 +
      true_params$m_beta1 * (temp - true_params$T_bar)
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(temp, truth_log10_lt50, bin_log10_lt50, bb_log10_lt50) |>
  dplyr::rename(
    `T (°C)`             = temp,
    `Truth log10(LT50)`  = truth_log10_lt50,
    `Binomial log10(LT50)` = bin_log10_lt50,
    `Beta-binomial log10(LT50)` = bb_log10_lt50
  )

tinytable::tt(stage1_table, digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

With the simulation's true asymptotes near 0 and 1, the two-parameter dose-response function forced-asymptote constraint introduces negligible bias here, so each Stage-1 estimate sits close to the simulation truth.

#### Stage 2 — log-linear TDT regression {#sec-twostage-step2}

Stage 2 regresses Stage-1 $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ on assay temperature with ordinary least squares. The classical TDT quantities — $z = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}} = (\log_{10} 60 - \alpha)/\beta_1$ — fall out of the fitted line.

```{r stage2-twostage}
#| label: stage2-twostage
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Regress log10(LT50) on temperature and derive z, CTmax and Tcrit.
stage2_bin <- ts_stage2(stage1_bin, t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1)
stage2_bb  <- ts_stage2(stage1_bb,  t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1)
```

@fig-stage2-regression visualises the Stage-2 fit. Each point is one Stage-1 $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ estimate from @tbl-stage1-lt50; the solid line is the OLS regression through them. The slope is $\beta_1$, from which $z = -1/\beta_1$ — i.e., the temperature increase that causes LT50 to change by a factor of 10. The line's height at $\log_{10} 60 \approx 1.78$ (1 hour) is at the temperature equal to $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ — the vertical dotted line drops from this intersection straight to the temperature axis to mark it. The dashed grey line is the simulation truth.

::: {#fig-stage2-regression}

```{r stage2-regression-plot}
#| label: plot-stage2-regression
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4.5

# Plot the Stage-2 regression and CTmax reference point.
stage1_combined <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  stage1_bin |> dplyr::mutate(dataset = "Binomial"),
  stage1_bb  |> dplyr::mutate(dataset = "Beta-binomial")
)

truth_line <- tibble::tibble(
  T = seq(min(temps), max(temps), length.out = 100)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log10_lt50 = true_params$m_beta0 +
      true_params$m_beta1 * (T - true_params$T_bar)
  )

ggplot2::ggplot(stage1_combined,
                ggplot2::aes(x = temp, y = log10_lt50, colour = dataset)) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = log10(60),
                      linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60") +
  ggplot2::geom_segment(
    ggplot2::aes(x = true_CTmax, xend = true_CTmax,
                 y = log10(60), yend = -Inf),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey60"
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(data = truth_line,
                     ggplot2::aes(x = T, y = log10_lt50),
                     inherit.aes = FALSE,
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40",
                     linewidth = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3) +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = max(temps), y = log10(60),
                    label = "log[10]~60~(1*' hour')", parse = TRUE,
                    hjust = 1, vjust = -0.4, colour = "grey50", size = 3) +
  ggplot2::annotate("text", x = true_CTmax, y = -Inf,
                    label = "CT[max[1*hr]]", parse = TRUE,
                    hjust = -0.15, vjust = -0.7, colour = "grey50", size = 3) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "Assay temperature (°C)",
    y = expression(log[10]~LT[50]~(min)),
    colour = "Stage-1 dataset"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

Stage-2 OLS regression of $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ on assay temperature, fit separately to the binomial and beta-binomial simulated datasets. Points are the Stage-1 per-temperature LT50 estimates; solid coloured lines are the OLS fits; the dashed grey line is the simulation truth. The slope of each line equals $\beta_1$, from which $z = -1/\beta_1$. The horizontal dotted line marks $\log_{10} 60$ (1 hour); the vertical dotted line drops from where the truth crosses 1 hour straight down to the temperature axis, marking $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ visually.
:::

Point estimates of $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ are than used as is often without uncertainty associated with these estimates. We compare these to the joint Bayesian results to show the 4PL gives the same values under these ideal conditions.

::: {.callout-important}
Classical two-stage analyses often treat Stage-1 $LT_{50}$ estimates as known when fitting Stage-2 models. In otherwords, uncertainty in $LT_{50}$ values are never accounted for in the Stage-2 regression. This can have consequences when estmating slopes and intercepts in Stage-2.

Despite being a potential issue, it is also challenging to correctly propagate error to $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ may have bigger impacts. Without propagating uncertainty properly these will systematically bias sampling uncertainty in $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ compromising inferences and leading to optimistic predictions. The Delta-method or bootstrap procedures can be used to propagate uncertainty, though, these are rarely used in practice.
:::

### *A joint Bayesian 4PL* {#sec-joint}

Instead of fitting separate logistic curves and then regressing their LT50 estimates, the joint fit estimates $\ell$, $\text{up}$, $k$, $\beta_0$, and $\beta_1$ simultaneously from the raw counts. We use the same simulated data to derive $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ directly from this model from the posteriors of these parameters.

#### Specifying the 4PL in `brms` {#sec-joint-spec}

The model has two pieces. The first is the 4PL itself — probability as a function of $\log_{10}$ exposure time:

$$
p \;=\; \ell \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \ell}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10} t - \text{mid})\bigr)},
$$ {#eq-supp-4pl}

where $\ell$ and $\text{up}$ are the lower and upper asymptotes, $k$ is the slope on the $\log_{10} t$ axis, and $\text{mid}$ is the value of $\log_{10} t$ at which the curve crosses the midpoint between $\ell$ and $\text{up}$. The second piece lets the midpoint vary linearly with mean-centred temperature:

$$
\text{mid} \;=\; \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar{T}),
$$ {#eq-supp-mid-temp}

so that $\beta_1$ encodes how fast the dose-response curve shifts on the time axis as assay temperature changes; $\bar{T}$ is the grand-mean temperature, included so that $\beta_0$ is interpretable as the midpoint at the average assay temperature.

The function library exposes this model as a single call: `fit_4pl()` constructs the brms `bf()` formula, the default weakly-informative priors, and the sampling controls, and returns a workflow object containing the fit. Internally the formula uses the disjoint-bounds reparameterisation described in §[Loading the function library] — `low = 0.001 + \text{inv\_logit}(\text{lowraw}) \cdot 0.498` and `up = 0.501 + \text{inv\_logit}(\text{upraw}) \cdot 0.498` — so the 4PL output is guaranteed to lie in $(0, 1)$ regardless of where MCMC takes the unconstrained `lowraw`, `upraw`, and `logk` parameters. The default also puts a `temp_c` slope on every 4PL sub-parameter; when temperature has no real effect on a given parameter, the slope shrinks toward zero under the prior. We inspect the brms formula `fit_4pl()` builds with:

```{r show-formula}
#| label: show-formula
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Print the non-linear 4PL brms formula used by fit_4pl().
make_4pl_formula()
```

::: {.callout-important}

The default model puts a `temp_c` slope on all four 4PL sub-parameters and includes no random effects. Random intercepts on `mid` can be added by passing `random_effects = c("Date", "Tank", ...)` to `fit_4pl()`. The simulation here has no batch structure so we leave it off; the shrimp case study below uses `random_effects = c("Date", "Tank")`.

:::

#### Standardising and fitting {#sec-joint-fit}

The first step is to pass the simulated data through `standardize_data()` so the columns match the schema the rest of the library expects (`temp`, `duration`, `logd`, `temp_c`, `n_total`, `n_surv`).

```{r standardise-sim}
#| label: standardise-sim
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Convert the simulated counts into the bayesTLS modelling schema.
std_bin <- standardize_data(sim_bin,
                            temp = "T", duration = "t",
                            n_total = "n", n_surv = "y",
                            duration_unit = "minutes")
std_bb  <- standardize_data(sim_bb,
                            temp = "T", duration = "t",
                            n_total = "n", n_surv = "y",
                            duration_unit = "minutes")
```

We now call `fit_4pl()` twice — once with the built-in `binomial(link = "identity")` family for the non-overdispersed simulation, and once with the default `beta_binomial(link = "identity")` for the overdispersed case. Caching is handled by brms's `file = ...` mechanism, so subsequent renders reload the cached fits unless the formula or data changes.

```{r fit-4pl-tutorial}
#| label: fit-4pl-tutorial
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Fit the tutorial 4PL models with and without overdispersion.
# Reuse cached fits; delete the .rds files to force refitting.
wf_bin <- fit_4pl(std_bin,
                  family = binomial(link = "identity"),
                  chains = 4, iter = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
                  file       = file.path(models_dir, "sim_4pl_binomial_v2"),
                  file_refit = "never")

wf_bb  <- fit_4pl(std_bb,
                  chains = 4, iter = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
                  file       = file.path(models_dir, "sim_4pl_betabinomial_v2"),
                  file_refit = "never")

# Show brms summaries and diagnostics for each fit.
summary(wf_bin)
summary(wf_bb)

# Extract the underlying brmsfit objects for brms helpers.
fit_bin <- get_brmsfit(wf_bin)
fit_bb  <- get_brmsfit(wf_bb)
```

We also summarise the proportion of outcome variation explained by each fitted model using Bayesian $R^2$. For any fitted 4PL this is a single `brms::bayes_R2()` call; we define a small helper that runs it per model and tidies the result into a `median [95% CrI]` table, then reuse it in every case study below.

```{r}
#| label: bayes-r2-helper
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Compute Bayesian R2 for each fitted model.
bayes_r2_table <- function(fits, digits = 3) {
  purrr::imap_dfr(fits, function(f, nm) {
    r <- brms::bayes_R2(f)                       # <- R^2 read straight from the fit
    tibble::tibble(
      Model         = nm,
      `Bayesian R²` = format_interval(r[1, "Estimate"], r[1, "Q2.5"],
                                      r[1, "Q97.5"], digits = digits)
    )
  })
}
```

```{r, r2_bayes}
#| label: tbl-r2-tutorial
#| tbl-cap: "Bayesian $R^2$ for the two tutorial joint 4PL fits (binomial and beta-binomial), read straight from `brms::bayes_R2()`. Cells are the posterior median with the 95% credible interval."
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Format Bayesian R2 for the tutorial fits.
bayes_r2_table(list(
  "Binomial 4PL"      = fit_bin,
  "Beta-binomial 4PL" = fit_bb
)) |> tinytable::tt(escape = TRUE)

```

#### Checking parameter recovery {#sec-joint-recovery}

We assess recovery by comparing posterior medians and 95% credible intervals with the known generating values (@tbl-recovery). Given the large simulated sample, the posterior should concentrate near those values.

```{r recovery-table}
#| label: tbl-recovery
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior medians and 95% credible intervals for the four 4PL parameters and the temperature slope, compared to the known truth used to generate the simulated data. Recovery is judged successful when the credible interval covers the truth."

# Format parameter recovery for the tutorial 4PL fits.
post_summary <- function(workflow) {
  # extract_4pl_pars() pulls the raw posterior and maps it back to the natural
  # 4PL scale using the model's own stored bounds (no hand-coded constants),
  # returning per-draw (low, up, k, mid_int, mid_temp). We rename to the
  # tutorial's labels and summarise each parameter.
  draws <- extract_4pl_pars(workflow) |>
    dplyr::transmute(ell = low, up = up, k = k,
                     `mid (intercept)` = mid_int, `mid (slope)` = mid_temp)
  purrr::map_dfr(names(draws), \(p) tibble::tibble(
    param  = p,
    median = stats::median(draws[[p]]),
    lower  = stats::quantile(draws[[p]], 0.025, names = FALSE),
    upper  = stats::quantile(draws[[p]], 0.975, names = FALSE)
  ))
}

truth <- tibble::tibble(
  param = c("ell", "up", "k", "mid (intercept)", "mid (slope)"),
  truth = c(true_params$ell, true_params$up, true_params$k,
            true_params$m_beta0, true_params$m_beta1)
)

recovery <- truth |>
  dplyr::left_join(post_summary(wf_bin) |>
                     dplyr::rename_with(~ paste0("bin_", .x), -param),
                   by = "param") |>
  dplyr::left_join(post_summary(wf_bb) |>
                     dplyr::rename_with(~ paste0("bb_", .x), -param),
                   by = "param") |>
  dplyr::rename(
    Parameter        = param,
    Truth            = truth,
    `Bin. median`    = bin_median,
    `Bin. lower`     = bin_lower,
    `Bin. upper`     = bin_upper,
    `BB median`      = bb_median,
    `BB lower`       = bb_lower,
    `BB upper`       = bb_upper
  )

tinytable::tt(recovery, digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

We can also overlay the posterior fitted curves on the simulated data (@fig-recovery-fit). If the model has recovered the true surface, the fitted lines should track the simulated proportions at every assay temperature.

::: {#fig-recovery-fit}

```{r recovery-fit-plot}
#| label: plot-recovery-fit
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

# Plot fitted survival curves against the simulated observations.
# Use the library's predict_survival_curves() and plot_survival_curves().
psc_bin <- predict_survival_curves(
  wf_bin, temps = temps,
  durations = 10 ^ seq(log10(0.5), log10(800), length.out = 100),
  ndraws = 200
)

plot_survival_curves(psc_bin, observed = std_bin) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_log10() +
  ggplot2::labs(x = expression(exposure~time~(min)))
```

Posterior fitted 4PL curves (solid lines, with shaded 95% credible bands) overlaid on the simulated binomial data (points). One panel-coloured line per assay temperature. Where the fitted curves track the simulated proportions across all five temperatures, the model has recovered the underlying dose-response surface.
:::

### *Deriving $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and $T_{crit}$ from the posterior* {#sec-joint-derive}

The classical TLS quantities are transformations of the fitted joint posterior. `extract_tdt()` always returns the first two quantities below and returns the third when `lethal = TRUE`:

- **$z$** — thermal sensitivity is read directly from the posterior as $z = -1/(\mathrm{d}\log_{10}\text{LT}_{\text{threshold}}/\mathrm{d}T)$ per draw (Equation 7 of the manuscript). Under the relative threshold, $\log_{10}\text{LT}(T) = \text{mid}(T)$ and the derivative is simply the linear slope, so $z = -1/\beta_1$ exactly.
- **$CT_{max_{1hr}}$** — temperature at which the threshold is reached after 1 h of exposure, obtained from the fitted 4PL at the reference time, per posterior draw.
- **$T_{crit}$** *(only when `lethal = TRUE`)* — the temperature below which damage accumulates more slowly than a chosen rate floor. The damage rate at temperature $T$ in the TDT framework is $r(T) = 100 \cdot 10^{(T - CT_{max,1hr})/z}$ % LT50-dose per hour, so inverting for a chosen rate floor $r^*$ gives

$$
T_{crit}(r^*) \;=\; CT_{max,1hr} \;+\; z \cdot \log_{10}(r^* / 100).
$$ {#eq-tcrit}

The threshold one wishes to use is set by the `target_surv` argument in `extract_tdt()`. Using `"relative"` evaluates at $(\text{low} + \text{up})/2$ per draw, which equals classical absolute 50% survival when asymptotes are anchored at 0 and 1 but accommodates sub-unit upper asymptotes (for example, background mortality unrelated to heat stress). Passing `target_surv = "absolute"` (the default) or any numeric value in $(0, 1)$ uses an absolute survival threshold. Because the simulated asymptotes are near 0 and 1, both definitions give essentially the same results here.

Different $r^*$ values imply different $T_{crit}$ thresholds, and the literature does not converge on one value. Empirical breakpoint analyses across taxa including *Drosophila suzukii* and *Lemna gibba* place $r^*$ in the range $[0.1, 1]$ % HI per hour, corresponding to approximately 100 to 1000 hours (about 4 to 42 days) of continuous exposure to reach $LT_{50}$ [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @arnold_thermal_2025; @faber2026]. We therefore integrate over a uniform prior on $\log_{10} r^*$ in that range. The resulting posterior for $T_{crit}$ includes parameter uncertainty in $CT_{max,1hr}$ and $z$ as well as operational uncertainty in $r^*$. Its median is $CT_{max,1hr} - 2.5 \cdot z$, corresponding to $r^* \approx 0.3$ % HI per hour.

::: {.callout-warning}
## $T_{crit}$ is meaningful only for lethal-endpoint data

The rate-multiplier $T_{crit}$ in Equation @eq-tcrit interprets $z$ as the temperature sensitivity of *damage accumulation*, which is appropriate for lethal-endpoint dose-response data and can be used to make a prediction about $T_{crit}$ because $T_{crit}$ should be the tempearture where damage begins to accumlate (0.1-1% is what we assume). `extract_tdt()` is therefore able to predict $T_{crit}$ with lethal data. When  `lethal = FALSE` `extract_tdt()` returns only $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. Users with lethal-endpoint data set can set `lethal = TRUE` to additionally derive $T_{crit}$. Note that this is only a prediction and does not negate the need to follow up with studies to validate [see @faber2026].

For **sublethal** endpoints fitted $z$ instead describes performance loss and can be very different from $z$ derived from lethal data. Substituting that value into Equation @eq-tcrit can shift $T_{crit}$ substantially and produce non-sensical $T_{crit}$ estimates. For example, a smaller sublethal $z$ can produce a $T_{crit}$ several °C higher than the lethal estimate despite a similar $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. Interpreting $T_{crit}$ as an intersection between thermal-performance and thermal-death-time curves requires both lethal and sublethal data [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022; @faber2026].
:::

Returned quantities (e.g., $CT_{max,1hr}$ and $z$) inherit the same posterior, so every credible interval reflects joint uncertainty in $(\ell, \text{up}, k, \beta_0, \beta_1)$. The opt-in $T_{crit}$ pools that same posterior with the additional uniform prior on $\log_{10} r^*$.

::: {.callout-note}

The default `fit_4pl()` puts a `temp_c` slope on every 4PL sub-parameter, so $\ell$, $\text{up}$ and $k$ can each carry a linear effect of temperature in addition to the linear-mid term — when those slopes shrink to zero the model reduces to the constant-shape case. However, when the data carry a real temperature signal on any of the shape parameters, the asymmetry correction $\tfrac{1}{k(T)}\log\tfrac{\text{up}(T)-0.5}{0.5-\ell(T)}$ varies with $T$ and the resulting $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve is bent rather than straight:

$$
\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T) \;=\; \beta_0 + \beta_1(T - \bar{T}) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{k(T)}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up}(T) - 0.5}{0.5 - \ell(T)}.
$$ {#eq-lt50-tvarying}

The correction factor is needed when calculating an 'absolute' threshold (e.g., 50% mortality - `target_surv = "absolute"`). Under an *absolute* threshold the asymmetry-correction term $\tfrac{1}{k(T)}\log\tfrac{\text{up}(T)-0.5}{0.5-\ell(T)}$ bends the curve when $\text{up}$, $\ell$ or $k$ carry $T$ effects; then a per-draw **local** $z(T) = -1/(\mathrm{d}\log_{10}\text{LT}_{0.5}/\mathrm{d}T)$ is computed at each assay temperature. The reported $z$ is the per-draw mean of those local values over the assay range — with the full per-temperature $z(T)$ available via `z_local = TRUE`.

If using a 'relative threshold' (`target_surv = "relative"`) then the calculations do not require the correction factor. $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ is the temperature at which $\log_{10}\text{LT}(T)$ crosses $t_\text{ref}$, per draw. For $z$, under the relative threshold $\log_{10}\text{LT}(T) = \text{mid}(T)$ is exactly linear, so $z = -1/\beta_1$ per draw.  The worked example in @sec-joint-extend demonstrates this with a simulation where `up` and $k$ carry strong $T$ effects.

The `extract_tdt()` function automatically applies the right correction factor by using the full posterior distribution for all parameters when using the `target_surv = "absolute"`. When subsampling to calculate $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ the posterior draws are taken once across all parameters to compute $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from that single set of draws. As such, the two quantities share draws and their correlation (and the joint pairing used for $T_{crit}$) is preserved.

:::

```{r derive-tls}
#| label: derive-tls
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Extract z, CTmax and Tcrit from the tutorial models.
# Use the 1-hour reference and request Tcrit because the endpoint is lethal.
et_bin <- extract_tdt(wf_bin, target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1,
                      ndraws = 1000, lethal = TRUE)
et_bb  <- extract_tdt(wf_bb,  target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1,
                      ndraws = 1000, lethal = TRUE)
```

```{r tls-recovery-table}
#| label: tbl-tls-recovery
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior medians and 95% credible intervals for thermal sensitivity ($z$), the critical thermal limit at a 1-hour reference duration ($CT_{max_{1hr}}$), and the rate-multiplier critical temperature ($T_{crit}$, integrated over $r^* \\in [0.1, 1]$ % HI per hour). All three derived via `extract_tdt()` from a single posterior. Each row's truth value is computed analytically from `true_params`."

# Format recovery of the derived TLS quantities.
# Pull median and interval columns from z or temperature summaries.
row_from_summary <- function(s, kind = c("z", "temp")) {
  kind <- match.arg(kind)
  if (kind == "z") {
    c(s$z_median, s$z_lower, s$z_upper)
  } else {
    c(s$temp_median, s$temp_lower, s$temp_upper)
  }
}

tls_recovery <- tibble::tibble(
  Quantity = c("z (°C)", "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)", "T_crit (°C)"),
  Truth    = c(true_z, true_CTmax, true_T_crit),
  `Bin. median` = c(row_from_summary(et_bin$z$summary,      "z")[1],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[1],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$T_crit$summary, "temp")[1]),
  `Bin. lower`  = c(row_from_summary(et_bin$z$summary,      "z")[2],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[2],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$T_crit$summary, "temp")[2]),
  `Bin. upper`  = c(row_from_summary(et_bin$z$summary,      "z")[3],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[3],
                    row_from_summary(et_bin$T_crit$summary, "temp")[3]),
  `BB median`   = c(row_from_summary(et_bb$z$summary,      "z")[1],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[1],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$T_crit$summary, "temp")[1]),
  `BB lower`    = c(row_from_summary(et_bb$z$summary,      "z")[2],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[2],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$T_crit$summary, "temp")[2]),
  `BB upper`    = c(row_from_summary(et_bb$z$summary,      "z")[3],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$CTmax$summary,  "temp")[3],
                    row_from_summary(et_bb$T_crit$summary, "temp")[3])
)

tinytable::tt(tls_recovery, digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

::: {#fig-tls-recovery}

```{r tls-recovery-plot}
#| label: plot-tls-recovery
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 3.5

# Plot posterior distributions of the recovered TLS quantities.
# Join posterior draws by `.draw` so quantities stay paired.
draws_bin_d <- get_z_draws(et_bin) |>
  dplyr::inner_join(get_ctmax_draws(et_bin), by = ".draw") |>
  dplyr::inner_join(get_tcrit_draws(et_bin), by = ".draw") |>
  dplyr::transmute(z, CTmax_1hr = CTmax, T_crit, model = "Binomial")
draws_bb_d <- get_z_draws(et_bb) |>
  dplyr::inner_join(get_ctmax_draws(et_bb),  by = ".draw") |>
  dplyr::inner_join(get_tcrit_draws(et_bb),  by = ".draw") |>
  dplyr::transmute(z, CTmax_1hr = CTmax, T_crit, model = "Beta-binomial")

post_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(draws_bin_d, draws_bb_d) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(c(z, CTmax_1hr, T_crit),
                      names_to = "quantity", values_to = "value") |>
  dplyr::mutate(quantity = factor(quantity,
                                  levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr", "T_crit"),
                                  labels = c("z (°C)",
                                             "CTmax_1hr (°C)",
                                             "T_crit (°C)")))

truth_long <- tibble::tibble(
  quantity = factor(c("z", "CTmax_1hr", "T_crit"),
                    levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr", "T_crit"),
                    labels = levels(post_long$quantity)),
  truth    = c(true_z, true_CTmax, true_T_crit)
)

ggplot2::ggplot(post_long, ggplot2::aes(value, fill = model)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(alpha = 0.5, colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(data = truth_long,
                      ggplot2::aes(xintercept = truth),
                      linetype = "dashed", colour = "black") +
  ggplot2::facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free") +
  ggplot2::labs(x = NULL, y = "Posterior density", fill = "Model") +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

Posterior densities of $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and $T_{crit}$ from the binomial (blue) and beta-binomial (orange) joint fits. Vertical dashed lines mark the simulation truth. All three posteriors concentrate around their respective truths, illustrating that a single call to `extract_tdt()` produces each classical TLS quantity with posterior uncertainty and no separate Stage-2 regression.
:::

Both fits recover all three simulation truths (@tbl-tls-recovery, @fig-tls-recovery), with credible intervals reflecting joint uncertainty in the 4PL parameters. In the joint approach, every classical TLS quantity is derived from the same fitted posterior.

Posterior summary statistics — mean, SD, median, and 95% credible interval — are also readily available from the `$draws` slot of `extract_tdt()`'s output:

```{r uncert-z-ct}
#| label: tbl-uncert-z-ct
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior summaries of $z$, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, and $T_{crit}$ from the joint Bayesian 4PL: posterior mean, standard deviation, median, and 95% credible interval, reported separately for the binomial and beta-binomial fits."
#| echo: true

# Compare uncertainty in z and CTmax across tutorial fits.
summarise_draws <- function(x) {
  q <- stats::quantile(x, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975), names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)
  tibble::tibble(mean = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE),
                 sd   = stats::sd(x, na.rm = TRUE),
                 median = q[2], lower = q[1], upper = q[3])
}

uncert_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  summarise_draws(get_z_draws(et_bin)$z)          |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "z (°C)",              likelihood = "Binomial"),
  summarise_draws(get_ctmax_draws(et_bin)$CTmax)  |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)", likelihood = "Binomial"),
  summarise_draws(get_tcrit_draws(et_bin)$T_crit) |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "T_crit (°C)",        likelihood = "Binomial"),
  summarise_draws(get_z_draws(et_bb)$z)           |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "z (°C)",              likelihood = "Beta-binomial"),
  summarise_draws(get_ctmax_draws(et_bb)$CTmax)   |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)", likelihood = "Beta-binomial"),
  summarise_draws(get_tcrit_draws(et_bb)$T_crit)  |> dplyr::mutate(quantity = "T_crit (°C)",        likelihood = "Beta-binomial")
) |>
  dplyr::select(quantity, likelihood, mean, sd, median, lower, upper)

# Scalars for inline reporting in the next paragraph.
sd_z_bin  <- round(stats::sd(get_z_draws(et_bin)$z),         2)
sd_z_bb   <- round(stats::sd(get_z_draws(et_bb)$z),          2)
sd_ct_bin <- round(stats::sd(get_ctmax_draws(et_bin)$CTmax), 2)
sd_ct_bb  <- round(stats::sd(get_ctmax_draws(et_bb)$CTmax),  2)
ci_z_bin  <- round(diff(stats::quantile(get_z_draws(et_bin)$z,         c(0.025, 0.975), names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)), 2)
ci_z_bb   <- round(diff(stats::quantile(get_z_draws(et_bb)$z,          c(0.025, 0.975), names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)), 2)
ci_ct_bin <- round(diff(stats::quantile(get_ctmax_draws(et_bin)$CTmax, c(0.025, 0.975), names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)), 2)
ci_ct_bb  <- round(diff(stats::quantile(get_ctmax_draws(et_bb)$CTmax,  c(0.025, 0.975), names = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)), 2)

uncert_summary |>
  dplyr::rename(
    Quantity   = quantity,
    Likelihood = likelihood,
    Mean       = mean,
    SD         = sd,
    Median     = median,
    Lower      = lower,
    Upper      = upper
  ) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

Both $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ are estimated precisely, and their credible intervals come directly from the joint posterior. A frequentist joint fit could similarly quantify transformation uncertainty using the Delta method or using bootstrap approaches.

Under the binomial likelihood, the posterior SD for $z$ is `r sd_z_bin` °C and its 95% credible interval is `r ci_z_bin` °C wide; $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ has SD `r sd_ct_bin` °C and interval width `r ci_ct_bin` °C.

The beta-binomial fit is slightly wider for both quantities (SD for $z$: `r sd_z_bb` °C; for $CT_{max_{1hr}}$: `r sd_ct_bb` °C) because $\phi$ captures extra-binomial variation and propagates it into the derived quantities. A two-stage pipeline requires an additional bootstrap or delta-method calculation to achieve comparable propagation.

::: {.callout-important}
For real data, it is important to compare binomial and beta-binomial fits rather than assuming either likelihood is adequate. When extra dispersion is present, the choice can affect uncertainty and inference.
:::

### *Comparing the two pipelines* {#sec-compare}

#### Point-estimate agreement on the same data {#sec-compare-table}

@tbl-twostage-compare puts the two-stage point estimates next to the joint Bayesian posterior medians for both simulated datasets.

```{r compare-twostage-table}
#| label: tbl-twostage-compare
#| tbl-cap: "Point estimates (two-stage) and posterior medians (joint Bayesian) for thermal sensitivity ($z$) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, recovered by each of four estimator-dataset combinations from the same simulated datasets. Truth values are the simulation parameters."

# Compare tutorial estimates from the joint and two-stage workflows.
compare_table <- tibble::tibble(
  quantity   = c("z (°C)", "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)"),
  truth      = c(true_z, true_CTmax),
  ts_bin     = c(stage2_bin$summary$z, stage2_bin$summary$CTmax_1hr),
  joint_bin  = c(median(draws_bin_d$z),
                 median(draws_bin_d$CTmax_1hr)),
  ts_bb      = c(stage2_bb$summary$z, stage2_bb$summary$CTmax_1hr),
  joint_bb   = c(median(draws_bb_d$z),
                 median(draws_bb_d$CTmax_1hr))
)

compare_table |>
  dplyr::rename(
    Quantity                          = quantity,
    Truth                             = truth,
    `Two-stage (binomial)`            = ts_bin,
    `Joint Bayesian (binomial)`       = joint_bin,
    `Two-stage (beta-binomial)`       = ts_bb,
    `Joint Bayesian (beta-binomial)`  = joint_bb
  ) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

All four estimators give similar point estimates for $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, close to the simulation truth. Under this simple generating scenario, the joint Bayesian 4PL reproduces the classical pipeline's central estimates while retaining a coherent posterior for downstream inference.

### *A worked example: temperature effects on every shape parameter* {#sec-joint-extend}

The default `fit_4pl()` places a `temp_c` slope on $\ell$, $\text{up}$, and $k$, estimating temperature effects on curve shape alongside the linear midpoint. To test recovery when the constant-shape assumption fails, we re-simulate beta-binomial data on the design in @sec-sim-step2 with `up` declining and $k$ increasing sharply with $T$. We then apply the same `fit_4pl()` and `extract_tdt()` calls used above.

```{r sim-data-ext}
#| label: sim-data-ext
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Prepare the extended simulation dataset for the package workflow example.
# Same factorial design as @sec-sim-step2; re-declared here so this chunk is
# self-contained.
temps     <- c(30, 32, 34, 36, 38)
durations <- c(1, 5, 15, 45, 135, 405)
n_rep     <- 30
n_per_rep <- 30
phi       <- 5

# Set truth on the raw parameter scale used by fit_4pl().
true_params_ext <- list(
  ell_raw  = qlogis(0.05 / 0.49),                 # ell = 0.05
  up_raw_0 = qlogis((0.92 - 0.51) / 0.49),        # up = 0.92 at T_bar
  up_raw_T = -0.60,                                # strong decline in up with T
  log_k_0  = log(8),                               # k = 8 at T_bar
  log_k_T  = 0.30,                                 # strong rise in k with T
  m_beta0  = 1.5,
  m_beta1  = -0.15,
  T_bar    = 34
)

design <- tidyr::expand_grid(
  T   = temps,
  t   = durations,
  rep = seq_len(n_rep)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log10_t = log10(t),
    T_c     = T - true_params_ext$T_bar,
    n       = n_per_rep
  )

sim_bb_ext <- design |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    ell    = 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$ell_raw),
    up     = 0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 +
                                   true_params_ext$up_raw_T * T_c),
    k      = exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 + true_params_ext$log_k_T * T_c),
    mid    = true_params_ext$m_beta0 + true_params_ext$m_beta1 * T_c,
    p_true = ell + (up - ell) / (1 + exp(k * (log10_t - mid))),
    p_draw = rbeta(dplyr::n(), p_true * phi, (1 - p_true) * phi),
    y      = rbinom(dplyr::n(), size = n, prob = p_draw)
  )
```

Here the lower asymptote remains at $\ell = 0.05$, while `up` falls from `r round(0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 + true_params_ext$up_raw_T * (30 - true_params_ext$T_bar)), 2)` at $T = 30$ to `r round(0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 + true_params_ext$up_raw_T * (38 - true_params_ext$T_bar)), 2)` at $T = 38$, and $k$ rises from `r round(exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 + true_params_ext$log_k_T * (30 - true_params_ext$T_bar)), 1)` to `r round(exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 + true_params_ext$log_k_T * (38 - true_params_ext$T_bar)), 1)`. The extreme-temperature curves therefore differ in shape, not merely position (@fig-true-surface-ext).

::: {#fig-true-surface-ext}

```{r true-surface-ext-plot}
#| label: plot-true-surface-ext
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

# Plot the true surface for the extended simulation example.
truth_grid_ext <- tidyr::expand_grid(
  T = temps,
  t = 10 ^ seq(log10(0.5), log10(800), length.out = 200)
) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    log10_t = log10(t),
    T_c     = T - true_params_ext$T_bar,
    ell     = 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$ell_raw),
    up      = 0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 +
                                   true_params_ext$up_raw_T * T_c),
    k       = exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 + true_params_ext$log_k_T * T_c),
    mid     = true_params_ext$m_beta0 + true_params_ext$m_beta1 * T_c,
    p_true  = ell + (up - ell) / (1 + exp(k * (log10_t - mid)))
  )

ggplot2::ggplot() +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed",
                      colour = "grey50") +
  ggplot2::geom_jitter(
    data = sim_bb_ext,
    ggplot2::aes(x = log10_t, y = y / n, colour = factor(T)),
    width = 0.02, height = 0, alpha = 0.25, size = 0.7
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(
    data = truth_grid_ext,
    ggplot2::aes(x = log10_t, y = p_true, colour = factor(T)),
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = expression(log[10]~exposure~time~(min)),
                y = "Survival probability",
                colour = "Temperature (°C)") +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

True 4PL dose-response surface (solid lines) for the extended simulation, where $k$ rises and `up` falls with assay temperature, overlaid on the simulated beta-binomial counts (points). Note how the curves at $T = 30$ and $T = 38$ have very different upper asymptotes and steepness — not just a horizontal shift along the time axis.
:::

Before fitting, we compute the analytical targets: $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$, local $z(T)$ (negative reciprocal of the local slope of $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ on $T$), and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (the temperature at which $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ crosses $\log_{10} 60$).

```{r truth-ext-derived}
#| label: truth-ext-derived
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Derive the true TLS quantities for the extended simulation example.
# True log10(LT50) at any T from the T-varying 4PL: solve p(log10_t) = 0.5
# for log10_t. With the 4PL,
#   log10_t = mid + (1/k) * log((up - 0.5) / (0.5 - ell))
true_log10_LT50 <- function(T_value) {
  T_c <- T_value - true_params_ext$T_bar
  ell <- 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$ell_raw)
  up  <- 0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 +
                              true_params_ext$up_raw_T * T_c)
  k   <- exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 + true_params_ext$log_k_T * T_c)
  mid <- true_params_ext$m_beta0 + true_params_ext$m_beta1 * T_c
  mid + (1 / k) * log((up - 0.5) / (0.5 - ell))
}

# Local z(T) via central finite difference of true log10(LT50)(T).
true_local_z <- function(T_at, half_step = 0.5) {
  -1 / ((true_log10_LT50(T_at + half_step) -
         true_log10_LT50(T_at - half_step)) / (2 * half_step))
}

# True CTmax_1hr: search the fine grid for the T at which log10(LT50)
# crosses log10(60).
T_grid_truth   <- seq(20, 45, by = 0.001)
ll_grid_truth  <- vapply(T_grid_truth, true_log10_LT50, numeric(1))
true_CTmax_1hr <- T_grid_truth[which.min(abs(ll_grid_truth - log10(60)))]

truth_ext <- tibble::tibble(
  T               = temps,
  log10_LT50_true = vapply(temps, true_log10_LT50, numeric(1)),
  LT50_min_true   = 10 ^ log10_LT50_true,
  local_z_true    = vapply(temps, true_local_z, numeric(1))
)

true_mean_local_z <- mean(truth_ext$local_z_true)
```

Across the assay range the true $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve is bent: the implied local $z$ varies from `r round(min(truth_ext$local_z_true), 2)` °C at $T = 30$ to `r round(max(truth_ext$local_z_true), 2)` °C at $T = 38$ (truth mean $z$ = `r round(true_mean_local_z, 2)` °C). The truth implies $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ = `r round(true_CTmax_1hr, 2)` °C. With straight-line $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ — the constant-shape case — local $z$ would not vary across temperature at all.

We now fit with exactly the same `fit_4pl()` call as before. The default formula already puts a `temp_c` slope on each of the four 4PL sub-parameters, so the model has the capacity to recover the truth's temperature signal on `up` and $k$ without any modification.

```{r fit-ext-default}
#| label: fit-ext-default
#| echo: true
#| cache: false
#| code-fold: show

# Fit the default package 4PL to the extended simulation data.
std_ext <- standardize_data(sim_bb_ext,
                            temp = "T", duration = "t",
                            n_total = "n", n_surv = "y",
                            duration_unit = "minutes")

wf_ext <- fit_4pl(std_ext,
                  chains = 4, iter = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 123,
                  file       = file.path(models_dir,
                                         "sim_4pl_ext_betabin_DEFAULT_strong_v1"),
                  file_refit = "never")     # see fit-4pl-tutorial note above
```

@tbl-ext-recovery reports recovery of the four 4PL sub-parameters on their natural scale at each assay temperature. The posterior keeps $\ell$ near $0.05$, while recovering the decline in `up` and increase in $k$ across the assay range.

```{r ext-recovery-table}
#| label: tbl-ext-recovery
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior recovery of the four 4PL sub-parameters on the natural scale at each assay temperature. The fit uses the disjoint-bounds reparameterisation ($\\ell$, $\\text{up}$ via inv_logit, $k$ via exp); raw posterior columns are transformed back to the biological scale before summarising. Posterior medians and 95% credible intervals are reported alongside the analytical truth."

# Format parameter recovery for the extended simulation example.
ext_post  <- posterior::as_draws_df(get_brmsfit(wf_ext)) |> as.data.frame()
ext_meta  <- wf_ext$meta
ext_bnds  <- ext_meta$bounds         # compute_4pl_bounds(0, 1)
ext_temp_mean <- ext_meta$temp_mean  # what the fit centred on

ext_param_long <- purrr::map_dfr(temps, function(T_value) {
  T_c_fit  <- T_value - ext_temp_mean
  T_c_true <- T_value - true_params_ext$T_bar

  draws_at_T <- tibble::tibble(
    ell = ext_bnds$low_min +
          stats::plogis(ext_post$b_lowraw_Intercept +
                        ext_post$b_lowraw_temp_c * T_c_fit) * ext_bnds$low_w,
    up  = ext_bnds$up_min  +
          stats::plogis(ext_post$b_upraw_Intercept +
                        ext_post$b_upraw_temp_c * T_c_fit)  * ext_bnds$up_w,
    k   = exp(ext_post$b_logk_Intercept + ext_post$b_logk_temp_c * T_c_fit),
    mid = ext_post$b_mid_Intercept + ext_post$b_mid_temp_c * T_c_fit
  )

  truth_at_T <- tibble::tibble(
    ell = 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$ell_raw),
    up  = 0.51 + 0.49 * plogis(true_params_ext$up_raw_0 +
                               true_params_ext$up_raw_T * T_c_true),
    k   = exp(true_params_ext$log_k_0 +
              true_params_ext$log_k_T * T_c_true),
    mid = true_params_ext$m_beta0 + true_params_ext$m_beta1 * T_c_true
  )

  purrr::map_dfr(c("ell", "up", "k", "mid"), function(p) {
    q <- stats::quantile(draws_at_T[[p]], c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975),
                         na.rm = TRUE, names = FALSE)
    tibble::tibble(
      Parameter         = p,
      `T (°C)`         = T_value,
      Truth             = truth_at_T[[p]],
      Median            = q[2],
      Lower             = q[1],
      Upper             = q[3]
    )
  })
})

ext_param_long |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

Now we apply the same `extract_tdt()` call as in @sec-joint-derive — there is no separate "T-varying" workflow because `extract_tdt()` already inverts the fitted surface numerically per draw:

```{r extract-tdt-ext}
#| label: extract-tdt-ext
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Extract TDT quantities from the extended simulation fit.
et_ext <- extract_tdt(wf_ext, target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60, time_multiplier = 1,
                      ndraws = 1000, lethal = TRUE)
```

@tbl-extract-ext compares the three classical TDT quantities from `extract_tdt()` to the analytical truth derived above.

```{r extract-tdt-ext-table}
#| label: tbl-extract-ext
#| tbl-cap: "Classical TDT quantities from `extract_tdt()` on the T-varying fit, compared against analytical truth. The single-summary $z$ is read directly from the posterior (under the relative threshold $z = -1/\\beta_1$ per draw — the slope of $\\text{mid}(T)$, with no regression); the truth row reports the mean of the analytical local $z(T)$ of the bent absolute-0.5 $\\log_{10}\\text{LT50}(T)$ curve at the assay temperatures. $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ comes from direct inversion of the fitted 4PL at 60 min per draw."

# Format extracted TDT quantities for the extended simulation fit.
tibble::tibble(
  Quantity     = c("z (°C)",
                   "CTmax at 1 hr (°C)",
                   "T_crit (°C)"),
  Truth        = c(round(true_mean_local_z, 3),
                   round(true_CTmax_1hr, 3),
                   NA_real_),  # T_crit truth involves r* integration; see below
  Median       = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_median,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_median,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_median),
  Lower        = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_lower,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_lower,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_lower),
  Upper        = c(et_ext$z$summary$z_upper,
                   et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_upper,
                   et_ext$T_crit$summary$temp_upper)
) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

Both posterior intervals cover their analytical truths. The single-summary $z$ read from the posterior ($-1/\beta_1$ per draw under the relative threshold) is close to the average local sensitivity of the bent curve, and its credible interval covers the truth (truth = `r round(true_mean_local_z, 2)` °C; posterior median `r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_median, 2)` °C, 95% CrI [`r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_lower, 2)`, `r round(et_ext$z$summary$z_upper, 2)`] °C). The bent absolute-threshold local $z(T)$ itself is available with `target_surv = "absolute", z_local = TRUE`. Direct 4PL inversion at the reference duration also recovers $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (truth `r round(true_CTmax_1hr, 2)` °C; posterior median `r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_median, 2)` °C, 95% CrI [`r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_lower, 2)`, `r round(et_ext$CTmax$summary$temp_upper, 2)`] °C), without assuming a constant asymmetry correction in $T$.

The bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve itself is the intermediate object both quantities are built from; it lives in `et_ext$lt50_curve` (the output of `derive_tdt_curve()`) and can be plotted directly (@fig-lt50-curve-ext).

::: {#fig-lt50-curve-ext}

```{r lt50-curve-ext-plot}
#| label: plot-lt50-curve-ext
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

# Plot the extended example LT50 curve.
truth_overlay <- {
  Ts <- seq(min(temps) - 2, max(temps) + 2, by = 0.05)
  tibble::tibble(temp = Ts,
                 duration_median = 10 ^ vapply(Ts, true_log10_LT50,
                                               numeric(1)))
}

plot_tdt_curve(et_ext$lt50_curve) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(data = truth_overlay,
                     ggplot2::aes(x = temp, y = duration_median),
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "black",
                     inherit.aes = FALSE)
```

Posterior $\text{LT50}(T)$ curve (median + 95% CrI, blue) recovered by `derive_tdt_curve()` from the T-varying fit, with the analytical truth overlaid (dashed black). The curve is visibly bent rather than straight in $T$, and the posterior tracks the truth.
:::

We can also drop down to the per-draw level and compute a *local* $z(T)$ at each assay temperature from the same `et_ext$lt50_curve` posterior — a finite difference of $\log_{10}\text{LT50}$ around $T$ per draw, summarised across draws (@fig-local-z-ext).

```{r local-z-ext}
#| label: local-z-ext
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: show

# Derive local z across the temperature range in the extended example.
# Finite-difference local z at each assay T, per posterior draw, derived
# from et_ext$lt50_curve (the same per-draw LT50(T) curve extract_tdt
# already computed for the OLS).
half_step <- 0.5

local_z_summary <- purrr::map_dfr(temps, function(T_at) {
  z_per_draw <- et_ext$lt50_curve$draws |>
    dplyr::group_by(.draw) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      z_local = -1 / ((stats::approx(temp, log10(duration_out),
                                     xout = T_at + half_step)$y -
                       stats::approx(temp, log10(duration_out),
                                     xout = T_at - half_step)$y) /
                      (2 * half_step)),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
  q <- stats::quantile(z_per_draw$z_local, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975),
                       na.rm = TRUE, names = FALSE)
  tibble::tibble(T_at           = T_at,
                 z_local_median = q[2],
                 z_local_lower  = q[1],
                 z_local_upper  = q[3])
})

local_z_summary |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    tibble::tibble(T_at = temps, z_local_truth = truth_ext$local_z_true),
    by = "T_at"
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(T_at, z_local_truth, z_local_median,
                z_local_lower, z_local_upper) |>
  dplyr::rename(
    `T (°C)`              = T_at,
    `z local truth (°C)`  = z_local_truth,
    Median                = z_local_median,
    Lower                 = z_local_lower,
    Upper                 = z_local_upper
  ) |>
  tinytable::tt(digits = 3, escape = TRUE)
```

::: {#fig-local-z-ext}

```{r local-z-ext-plot}
#| label: plot-local-z-ext
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4

# Plot how local z changes across temperature in the extended example.
truth_curve_z <- {
  Ts <- seq(min(temps), max(temps), by = 0.1)
  tibble::tibble(T_at = Ts,
                 z_local_truth = vapply(Ts, true_local_z, numeric(1)))
}

ggplot2::ggplot(local_z_summary,
                ggplot2::aes(x = T_at, y = z_local_median)) +
  ggplot2::geom_ribbon(ggplot2::aes(ymin = z_local_lower,
                                    ymax = z_local_upper),
                       fill = "#146C7C", alpha = 0.2) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(colour = "#146C7C", linewidth = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_line(data = truth_curve_z,
                     ggplot2::aes(x = T_at, y = z_local_truth),
                     linetype = "dashed", colour = "black",
                     inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Assay temperature (°C)",
                y = expression(local~italic(z)~"(°C per 10-fold change in time)")) +
  ggplot2::theme_classic()
```

Local $z(T)$ recovered from the per-draw $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve (blue median + 95% CrI), with the analytical truth overlaid (dashed black). Where the constant-shape simple-case fit would give the same $z$ at every assay temperature, here local $z$ varies with $T$ in lock-step with the bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve.
:::

The same `fit_4pl()` and `extract_tdt()` calls recover the bent $\log_{10}\text{LT50}(T)$ curve, average $z$ across the assay grid, and per-draw $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. Under the constant-shape truth in @sec-joint-derive, near-zero slopes on `up`, $\ell$, and $k$ reduce this calculation to the closed-form case.

### Heat injury accumulation and survival under a fluctuating trace
