```{r sim-supp-setup}
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# Add T_bar after inversion so CTmax is on the original temperature scale.
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

.quantity_labeller <- as_labeller(c(
  z          = "z (°C)",
  CTmax_1hr  = "CT[max,1h] (°C)"
))

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))
  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)
  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario","sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb","two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      rmse      = sqrt(mean(bias^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    ) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method_code = as.character(method),
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("CTmax_1hr","z")),
      bias_lo  = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi  = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      cov_lo   = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
      cov_hi   = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
    )
}

# Count failed or implausible simulations by method and scenario.
.sim_fail_cache <- new.env(parent = emptyenv())
.sim_fail <- function(prefix, m) {
  if (is.null(.sim_fail_cache[[prefix]])) {
    files <- list.files(
      here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
      pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"), full.names = TRUE)
    .sim_fail_cache[[prefix]] <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS)) |>
      dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, sim_id) |>
      dplyr::summarise(bad = any(!success) |
                             any(quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                             any(quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10),
                       .groups = "drop") |>
      dplyr::group_by(scenario, method) |>
      dplyr::summarise(n = dplyr::n(), n_bad = sum(bad),
                       pct = 100 * mean(bad), .groups = "drop")
  }
  x <- dplyr::filter(.sim_fail_cache[[prefix]], method == m)
  if (nrow(x) == 0 || max(x$n_bad) == 0) return("0")
  lo <- min(x$n_bad); hi <- max(x$n_bad)
  plo <- min(x$pct);  phi <- max(x$pct)
  if (lo == hi) sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", hi, phi)
  else sprintf("%d–%d (%.1f–%.1f%%)", lo, hi, plo, phi)
}

# Forest plot for cell-based scenarios (Scen 1, 2, 4, 8).
# `panel_var` is the column name to facet rows on (e.g. "panel_n" or "cell").
.plot_forest <- function(d, panel_var = "panel_n") {
  p_bias <- ggplot(d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    facet_grid(reformulate("quantity", panel_var), scales = "free_x",
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL,
         title = "a) Bias") +
    .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")
  p_cov <- ggplot(d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    facet_grid(reformulate("quantity", panel_var), scales = "free_x",
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
    labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
         title = "b) Coverage") +
    .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")
  p_bias / p_cov + plot_layout(heights = c(1, 1))
}

# Sweep coverage plot for Scen 6 / Scen 7.
.plot_sweep_cov <- function(d, sweep_x, x_label, x_breaks = waiver()) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = .data[[sweep_x]], y = coverage, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = cov_lo, ymax = cov_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, nrow = 1,
               labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "95% CI coverage") +
    .theme_proj
}

# Build the RMSE table for z and CTmax.
.rmse_table <- function(d, cell_name = "Cell") {
  pe <- c(two_stage_bin = "Two-stage (binomial)",
          two_stage_bb  = "Two-stage (beta-binomial)",
          joint_4pl     = "Joint 4PL (relative)",
          joint_4pl_abs = "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
  out <- d |>
    dplyr::filter(method_code %in% names(pe)) |>
    dplyr::transmute(Cell, Method = factor(pe[method_code], levels = pe),
                     quantity = as.character(quantity), rmse = round(rmse, 2)) |>
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = quantity, values_from = rmse) |>
    dplyr::arrange(Cell, Method) |>
    dplyr::select(Cell, Method, `z (°C)` = z, `CTmax (°C)` = CTmax_1hr)
  names(out)[1] <- cell_name
  # Escape underscores and other LaTeX-sensitive table text.
  tinytable::tt(out) |> tinytable::format_tt(escape = TRUE)
}
```

## *Strict equivalence baseline*

This scenario is the strict-equivalence baseline. The data-generating curve spans the full survival range ($\text{up} = 0.999$, $\text{low} = 0.001$), has a shared slope ($k = 8$), and has a midpoint that declines linearly with temperature (slope $-0.15$) under a binomial likelihood with no overdispersion. We crossed this scenario with two replicate designs, $n_{\text{reps}} \in \{3, 5\}$, with $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ datasets per cell.

We included this scenario to check whether the estimators agree when the classical two-stage assumptions are satisfied. As expected, all methods recovered $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ with little bias. The main difference was interval calibration: two-stage intervals based on Normal quantiles did not have nominal coverage, whereas the *t*-quantile delta-method intervals were close to the nominal 95% coverage. Again, it is important to recognise that we have ensured error from the two-stage is correctly propagated for comparison but often this is ignored.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias — here and throughout, $|z|$ bias above 20 °C or $|CT_{max,1h}|$ bias above 10 °C, far outside any plausible value — for `r .sim_fail("scen1", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen1", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen1", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations. The overdispersion-corrected two-stage thus fails to converge on a sensible estimate even in this most benign scenario, whereas the joint 4PL does not.


```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen1
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "Bias and coverage at the strict-equivalence baseline (binomial DGP, asymptotes near 0/1, single slope $k$). Rows: $n_{reps}=3$ (top) and $n_{reps}=5$ (bottom). Columns: $z$ (left) and $CT_{max,1h}$ (right). Dashed reference lines at zero bias and nominal 0.95 coverage."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 1.
.d <- .load_screened("scen1") |>
  dplyr::mutate(panel_n = paste0("n[reps] = ",
                                  sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)))
.plot_forest(.d, panel_var = "panel_n")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen1-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ at the strict-equivalence baseline, by point estimator and replication budget ($N_{sim}=1000$ per cell). Lower is better; RMSE combines bias and precision on the parameter's own scale."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 1.
.load_screened("scen1") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Cell = factor(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario),
                              levels = c("3", "5"))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "n_reps")
```

## *Likelihood misspecification alone*

This scenario keeps the same mean dose-response shape as the strict-equivalence baseline, but generates counts from a beta-binomial likelihood with overdispersion $\phi = 5$. We again used two replicate budgets, $n_{\text{reps}} \in \{3, 5\}$, with $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ datasets per cell.

We included this scenario to isolate the cost of likelihood misspecification: the mean structure is correct for all estimators, but the binomial two-stage variant ignores overdispersion at Stage 1. Point estimates remained largely unbiased, showing that overdispersion alone did not shift the target estimands. The effect was on uncertainty rather than location, and it was modest here: overdispersion alone barely separated the binomial and beta-binomial two-stage fits, in either point estimates or calibration. Both produced Normal-quantile intervals that under-covered and were brought close to nominal by the *t*-quantile correction, while the joint 4PL was well calibrated throughout.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen2", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen2", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen2", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "Bias and coverage with likelihood misspecification only (beta-binomial DGP with $\\phi=5$; curve shape as in the strict-equivalence baseline). Rows: $n_{reps}=3$ (top) and $n_{reps}=5$ (bottom)."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 2.
.d <- .load_screened("scen2") |>
  dplyr::mutate(panel_n = paste0("n[reps] = ",
                                  sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)))
.plot_forest(.d, panel_var = "panel_n")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen2-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ under likelihood misspecification only (beta-binomial DGP, $\\phi=5$; curve shape as in the baseline), by point estimator and replication budget."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 2.
.load_screened("scen2") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Cell = factor(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario),
                              levels = c("3", "5"))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "n_reps")
```

## *Asymptotes compress symmetrically*

In this scenario, both asymptotes move toward each other as temperature increases: `up` declines by 0.01 per °C from 0.92, while $\text{low}$ increases by 0.01 per °C from 0.05. This preserves the approximate logit-difference but compresses the response range. Although this symmetric compression is not biologically realistic, it is useful as a diagnostic test: it changes the dose-response range while largely preserving the midpoint-temperature relationship. We used two replicate budgets, $n_{\text{reps}} \in \{3, 5\}$, with $N_{\text{sim}} = 1000$ datasets per cell.

The two-stage fits showed clear bias here — negative in $CT_{max,1h}$ and positive in $z$ — together with under-coverage, because the classical model cannot represent changing asymptotes directly. The joint 4PL remained close to unbiased with near-nominal coverage.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen4", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen4", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen4", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen4
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "Bias and coverage when both asymptotes compress symmetrically with temperature. Rows: $n_{reps}=3$ (top) and $n_{reps}=5$ (bottom)."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 4.
.d <- .load_screened("scen4") |>
  dplyr::mutate(panel_n = paste0("n[reps] = ",
                                  sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)))
.plot_forest(.d, panel_var = "panel_n")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen4-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ when both asymptotes compress symmetrically with temperature, by point estimator and replication budget."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 4.
.load_screened("scen4") |>
  dplyr::mutate(Cell = factor(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario),
                              levels = c("3", "5"))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "n_reps")
```

## *Coverage for drifting `up` across temperatures ($\beta_{up}$)*

This complements the main-text $\beta_{up}$ bias sweep with the matching
coverage panels.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen6", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen6", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen6", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen6cov
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-cap: "95% CI coverage across the $\\beta_{up}$ sweep (Scen 6). Left: $z$. Right: $CT_{max,1h}$. Dashed line at nominal 0.95. Same DGP and method set as the main-text $\\beta_{up}$ bias sweep, with coverage shown for all six methods. Normal- and t-quantile two-stage CIs differ in coverage even though their point estimates are identical."

# Plot coverage for simulation scenario 6.
.d <- .load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 1000)
.plot_sweep_cov(.d, "sweep_x",
                expression(beta[up]~"(percentage points / °C, drift of "*plain(up)*" with T)"),
                x_breaks = c(-0.019, -0.015, -0.010, -0.005))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen6-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ across the $\\beta_{up}$ drift sweep (Scen 6), by point estimator. $\\beta_{up}$ is the per-°C drift of the upper asymptote (percentage points per °C)."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 6.
.load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(bu   = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 1000,
                Cell = factor(sprintf("%.3f", bu),
                              levels = sprintf("%.3f", sort(unique(bu))))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "beta_up (pp/°C)")
```

## *Coverage for constant-but-reduced `up` ($\text{up}_0$)*

This complements the main-text $\text{up}_0$ bias sweep. With a low $\text{up}_0$, the
two-stage Stage-1 GLMs miscalibrate per temperature, the Stage-2 OLS
inherits that bias, and CI coverage on $CT_{max,1h}$ collapses well
below nominal even after the t-quantile small-sample correction.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen7", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen7", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen7", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen7cov
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-cap: "95% CI coverage across the $\\text{up}_0$ sweep (Scen 7). Same DGP and method set as the main-text $\\text{up}_0$ bias sweep. Two-stage CTmax coverage collapses as $\\text{up}_0$ falls; the t-correction widens CIs without recovering nominal calibration. Joint 4PL coverage holds at 92–96% throughout."

# Plot coverage for simulation scenario 7.
.d <- .load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100)
.plot_sweep_cov(.d, "sweep_x",
                expression(up[0]~"(constant upper asymptote, all T)"),
                x_breaks = c(0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen7-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ across the $\\text{up}_0$ sweep (Scen 7), by point estimator. $\\text{up}_0$ is the constant upper asymptote held across all temperatures."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 7.
.load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(u0   = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100,
                Cell = factor(sprintf("%.2f", u0),
                              levels = sprintf("%.2f", sort(unique(u0))))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "up_0")
```

## *Experimental Design Impacts*

#### *Different temperature time duration levels*
This scenario varies experimental design while holding the data-generating process fixed. We crossed two temperature-duration grids (full: 5 temperatures × 6 durations; sparse: 3 temperatures × 4 durations) with three levels of replication, $n_{\text{reps}} \in \{1, 3, 5\}$. Here, $n_{\text{reps}}$ is the number of replicate cups per temperature-by-duration cell; each cup contained $N \sim \text{Uniform}\{10,\ldots,20\}$ organisms. The DGP used the baseline beta-binomial shape ($\text{up} = 0.92$, $\text{low} = 0.05$, $k = 8$, midpoint linear in $T$, $\phi = 5$); the sparsest design had only 12 'cups' total.

We included this scenario to test how much information each estimator needs before the two-stage reduction becomes unstable. Performance improved with denser designs and more replication, but the sparse one-replicate design was especially problematic for the two-stage pipeline. The joint 4PL was more stable across the design gradient because it shares information across temperatures and durations rather than estimating each temperature-specific curve separately.

Of the 1,000 datasets per cell, models in the two-stage analytical approach failed to fit or resulted in extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen8", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen8", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) Stage-1 fits, versus `r .sim_fail("scen8", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL, across the simulations.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen8
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 11.5
#| fig-height: 9.6
#| fig-dpi: 300
#| fig-cap: "Bias and coverage across six design × replication cells (Scen 8). Two-stage at sparse × 1 produces large, unstable estimates with implausibly wide t-corrected CIs; joint 4PL returns coherent intervals of appropriate width across all six cells."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 8.
.d <- .load_screened("scen8") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    design = ifelse(grepl("full", scenario), "full", "sparse"),
    n_reps = as.integer(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
    cell   = factor(sprintf("%s × %d", design, n_reps),
                    levels = c("sparse × 1", "full × 1",
                               "sparse × 3", "full × 3",
                               "sparse × 5", "full × 5"))
  )

p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = cell, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi),
                 height = 0.25, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x",
             labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL,
       title = "a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj

p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = cell, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi),
                 height = 0.25, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x",
             labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
  labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
       title = "b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

p_bias / p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen8-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ across the six design × replication cells (Scen 8), by point estimator. The sparse, single-replicate design is the most demanding for the two-stage reduction."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 8.
.load_screened("scen8") |>
  dplyr::mutate(design = ifelse(grepl("full", scenario), "full", "sparse"),
                n_reps = as.integer(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                Cell   = factor(sprintf("%s x %d", design, n_reps),
                                levels = c("sparse x 1", "full x 1",
                                           "sparse x 3", "full x 3",
                                           "sparse x 5", "full x 5"))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "Design x reps")
```

#### *Performance when estimating under short timeframes*

Thermal-tolerance assays are often limited by how long they can run. Long exposures at cooler assay temperatures are costly, so experiments may stop before those treatments reach their LT50. This scenario isolates that cost. We held the data-generating process fixed at the baseline beta-binomial shape ($\text{up} = 0.92$, $\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, midpoint linear in $T$, $\phi=5$), used the full five-temperature design (30–38 °C) with $n_{\text{reps}} = 5$, and varied only the maximum exposure duration. Each design used six log-spaced durations capped at 60, 120, 240, or 405 min; the 405-min cap reproduces the full grid. Because the truth is identical across caps, changes in bias, coverage, and RMSE reflect only how well each design recovers the same $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$.

Shorter windows remove the long-duration observations that anchor the cooler-temperature LT50s. At the shortest caps, the coolest temperatures may never reach 50% mortality, so the LT50 must be extrapolated rather than observed. This scenario therefore tests whether each estimator degrades gracefully as duration information is withdrawn, and whether the joint 4PL retains calibration better than the two-stage reduction by borrowing strength across temperatures.

Across the 1,000 datasets per cap, the two-stage Stage-1 fits failed or produced extreme bias for `r .sim_fail("scen9", "two_stage_bin")` binomial and `r .sim_fail("scen9", "two_stage_bb")` beta-binomial (glmmTMB) fits, compared with `r .sim_fail("scen9", "joint_4pl")` for the joint 4PL.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-supp-scen9
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 11.5
#| fig-height: 8.2
#| fig-dpi: 300
#| fig-cap: "Effect of maximum exposure duration on estimator performance (Scen 9). Rows show mean bias (a; estimate minus truth) and 95% interval coverage (b) for $z$ and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. The 405-min cap is the full design; shorter caps progressively remove the long-duration observations that anchor LT50 at cooler assay temperatures."

# Plot bias and coverage for simulation scenario 9.
# Parse maximum exposure duration from the scenario name.
.d <- .load_screened("scen9") |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    tmax = as.integer(sub(".*_tmax_([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
    cell = factor(sprintf("%d min", tmax),
                  levels = c("60 min", "120 min", "240 min", "405 min"))
  )

# Use shorter legend labels locally; the colour values are still taken from the
# shared simulation palette defined in the setup chunk.
.method_short <- c(
  "Two-stage bin (Normal CI)" = "2-stage bin, normal",
  "Two-stage bin (t CI)"      = "2-stage bin, t",
  "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)"  = "2-stage BB, normal",
  "Two-stage BB (t CI)"       = "2-stage BB, t",
  "Joint 4PL (relative)"      = "Joint 4PL, relative",
  "Joint 4PL (absolute)"      = "Joint 4PL, absolute"
)

# Panel (a): Monte Carlo mean bias with 95% Monte Carlo SE intervals.
p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = cell, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi),
                 height = 0.25, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x",
             labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = "Estimator",
                      values = .method_cols,
                      breaks = names(.method_short),
                      labels = unname(.method_short)) +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = "Maximum exposure",
       title = "a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj

# Panel (b): empirical coverage; the dashed line marks the nominal 95% target.
p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = cell, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi),
                 height = 0.25, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2, position = position_dodge(width = 0.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x",
             labeller = labeller(quantity = .quantity_labeller)) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = "Estimator",
                      values = .method_cols,
                      breaks = names(.method_short),
                      labels = unname(.method_short)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95),
                     labels = c("0", "0.50", "0.95 target")) +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE)) +
  labs(x = "Empirical coverage", y = "Maximum exposure",
       title = "b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

# Stack the panels and collect one shared, readable estimator legend.
p_bias / p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-sim-supp-scen9-rmse
#| tbl-cap: "RMSE (°C) of $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ by maximum exposure duration (Scen 9). Lower RMSE indicates more accurate point estimates. The 60-min cap is the most demanding because the coolest assay temperatures may not reach 50% mortality within the observation window."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
# Format the RMSE table for simulation scenario 9.
.load_screened("scen9") |>
  dplyr::mutate(tmax = as.integer(sub(".*_tmax_([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                Cell = factor(sprintf("%d min", tmax),
                              levels = c("60 min", "120 min",
                                         "240 min", "405 min"))) |>
  .rmse_table(cell_name = "Max exposure")
```


# **Manuscript Figure 5: cross-case-study summary** {#sec-ms-fig4}

This figure compiles the joint Bayesian 4PL posterior distributions of the TDT quantities ($z$ and $CT_{max}$) from the four case studies into a single multi-panel display. The zebrafish row splits into three ridges (one per life stage) and the vinegar fly row into two (female, male). The $CT_{max}$ reference exposure follows each case study's own analysis — 1 h for shrimp, zebrafish and leaf, 4 h for the vinegar fly — so the right-column strips are labelled $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ and $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ respectively; the panels carry independent x-axes, so the columns are not on a shared scale in any case.

```{r ms-fig4-case-study-densities}
#| label: fig-ms-fig4-case-study-densities
#| fig-cap: "Manuscript Figure 5. Posterior distributions of thermal sensitivity $z$ (left) and the critical thermal limit $CT_{max}$ (right) from the joint Bayesian 4PL fits for four case studies: brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*), snow gum leaf PSII (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*), zebrafish (*Danio rerio*; three life stages, panel c) and the vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*; by sex, panel d). Filled ridges are the posterior densities, coloured by species and level; the black-outlined point and bar give the posterior median and 95% credible interval, also printed beside each ridge. The $CT_{max}$ reference exposure follows each study’s own analysis: 1 h for shrimp, zebrafish and leaf (relative midpoint threshold) and 4 h for the vinegar fly (absolute LT50, following @orsted_suzukii_2024). For the zebrafish life stages and fly sexes, pairwise posterior contrasts are annotated as brackets labelled with the pMCMC (twice the smaller posterior tail probability of the difference; smaller values indicate stronger evidence the groups differ). Panels carry independent x-axes."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
#| cache: false

# Assemble the manuscript cross-case posterior-density figure.
# 4PL posterior draws (one row per draw, per case study × group × quantity).
fig4_draws <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Brown shrimp", group = "Brown shrimp",
    quantity   = "z",         value = get_z_draws(et_shrimp)$z,
    .draw      = get_z_draws(et_shrimp)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Brown shrimp", group = "Brown shrimp",
    quantity   = "CTmax_1hr", value = get_ctmax_draws(et_shrimp)$CTmax,
    .draw      = get_ctmax_draws(et_shrimp)$.draw
  ),
  dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(zf_stages, function(s) {
    dr <- zf_joint_tdt[[s]]$z$draws  # hand-packaged list (built via tls()): .draw, z, CTmax
    dplyr::bind_rows(
      tibble::tibble(case_study = "Zebrafish", group = s,
                     quantity = "z",         value = dr$z,     .draw = dr$.draw),
      tibble::tibble(case_study = "Zebrafish", group = s,
                     quantity = "CTmax_1hr", value = dr$CTmax, .draw = dr$.draw)
    )
  })),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Snow gum leaf PSII", group = "Snow gum leaf PSII",
    quantity   = "z",         value = get_z_draws(et_leaf)$z,
    .draw      = get_z_draws(et_leaf)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Snow gum leaf PSII", group = "Snow gum leaf PSII",
    quantity   = "CTmax_1hr", value = get_ctmax_draws(et_leaf)$CTmax,
    .draw      = get_ctmax_draws(et_leaf)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Vinegar fly (mortality)", group = "Female",
    quantity   = "z",         value = get_z_draws(tdt_dros_f)$z,
    .draw      = get_z_draws(tdt_dros_f)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Vinegar fly (mortality)", group = "Female",
    quantity   = "CTmax_4hr", value = get_ctmax_draws(tdt_dros_f)$CTmax,
    .draw      = get_ctmax_draws(tdt_dros_f)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Vinegar fly (mortality)", group = "Male",
    quantity   = "z",         value = get_z_draws(tdt_dros_m)$z,
    .draw      = get_z_draws(tdt_dros_m)$.draw
  ),
  tibble::tibble(
    case_study = "Vinegar fly (mortality)", group = "Male",
    quantity   = "CTmax_4hr", value = get_ctmax_draws(tdt_dros_m)$CTmax,
    .draw      = get_ctmax_draws(tdt_dros_m)$.draw
  )
) |>
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(value))

# Posterior median + 95% credible interval from the joint 4PL, one row per
# case × group × quantity (overlaid on the ridge as a pointrange).
mk_row <- function(cs, grp, q, med, lo, hi) {
  tibble::tibble(case_study = cs, group = grp, quantity = q,
                 median = med, lower = lo, upper = hi)
}

zf_rows <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(zf_stages, function(s) {
  z_sum <- zf_joint_tdt[[s]]$z$summary
  dplyr::bind_rows(
    mk_row("Zebrafish", s, "z",         z_sum$z_median,     z_sum$z_lower,     z_sum$z_upper),
    mk_row("Zebrafish", s, "CTmax_1hr", z_sum$CTmax_median, z_sum$CTmax_lower, z_sum$CTmax_upper)
  )
}))

fig4_summary <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  mk_row("Brown shrimp", "Brown shrimp", "z",
         et_shrimp$z$summary$z_median,
         et_shrimp$z$summary$z_lower,
         et_shrimp$z$summary$z_upper),
  mk_row("Brown shrimp", "Brown shrimp", "CTmax_1hr",
         et_shrimp$CTmax$summary$temp_median,
         et_shrimp$CTmax$summary$temp_lower,
         et_shrimp$CTmax$summary$temp_upper),
  zf_rows,
  mk_row("Snow gum leaf PSII", "Snow gum leaf PSII", "z",
         et_leaf$z$summary$z_median,
         et_leaf$z$summary$z_lower,
         et_leaf$z$summary$z_upper),
  mk_row("Snow gum leaf PSII", "Snow gum leaf PSII", "CTmax_1hr",
         et_leaf$CTmax$summary$temp_median,
         et_leaf$CTmax$summary$temp_lower,
         et_leaf$CTmax$summary$temp_upper),
  # Vinegar fly mortality, by sex. 4PL summaries (z_f/z_m, ctmax4_f/ctmax4_m)
  # come from the extract_tdt() chunk above; CTmax is the 4 h value.
  mk_row("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Female", "z",
         z_f$z_median, z_f$z_lower, z_f$z_upper),
  mk_row("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Female", "CTmax_4hr",
         ctmax4_f$temp_median, ctmax4_f$temp_lower, ctmax4_f$temp_upper),
  mk_row("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Male", "z",
         z_m$z_median, z_m$z_lower, z_m$z_upper),
  mk_row("Vinegar fly (mortality)", "Male", "CTmax_4hr",
         ctmax4_m$temp_median, ctmax4_m$temp_lower, ctmax4_m$temp_upper)
)

# Persist the per-group z/CTmax medians so the manuscript can report figures
# (e.g. the CTmax_1hr range across case studies) as inline code, not magic
# numbers. Read back in ms/ms.qmd.
dir.create(here::here("output", "data"), showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
saveRDS(fig4_summary, here::here("output", "data", "fig4_summary.rds"))

# Manuscript Figure 5 design settings.
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(ggplot2); library(dplyr); library(tibble); library(ggridges)
  library(patchwork); library(ggtext); library(cowplot); library(magick); library(here)
})

# Facet order: z (left), CTmax (right); fly uses the 4 h CTmax reference.
fig4_draws   <- mutate(fig4_draws,   quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr", "CTmax_4hr")))
fig4_summary <- mutate(fig4_summary, quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr", "CTmax_4hr")))

# Colour by species / level; the CI bar + point use a darker tone of the fill.
vz <- viridisLite::viridis(3, begin = 0.15, end = 0.85)              # zebrafish stages
fig4_pal <- c("Brown shrimp" = "#C8732B", "Snow gum leaf PSII" = "#4F7942",
              setNames(vz, zf_stages), "Female" = "#009E73", "Male" = "#E69F00")
darken <- function(col, f = 0.34) { v <- grDevices::col2rgb(col) / 255 * (1 - f); grDevices::rgb(v[1], v[2], v[3]) }
fig4_pal_dark <- sapply(fig4_pal, darken)
qlab <- as_labeller(c(z = "italic(z)~(degree*C)", CTmax_1hr = "CT[max[1*hr]]~(degree*C)",
                      CTmax_4hr = "CT[max[4*hr]]~(degree*C)"), default = label_parsed)
P <- function(x) here("pics", x)

# Patrice's v2 illustrations are vector SVGs. Rasterise each to a high-resolution
# temporary PNG so the 600-dpi figure stays crisp and the existing
# draw_image()/<img> paths (which expect a raster file) work unchanged. Cached so
# each SVG is rasterised once per session.
svg_png <- local({
  cache <- new.env(parent = emptyenv())
  function(svg, width = 1600) {
    key <- paste0(svg, "@", width)
    if (is.null(cache[[key]])) {
      o <- tempfile(fileext = ".png")
      magick::image_write(magick::image_read_svg(P(svg), width = width), o)
      cache[[key]] <- o
    }
    cache[[key]]
  }
})

# Trim white margins so the danio stage illustrations are size-comparable.
danio_src  <- c(young_embryos = "danio_early_v2.svg", old_embryos = "danio_late_embryo_v2.svg", larvae = "danio_larvae_v2.svg")
danio_trim <- vapply(danio_src, function(f) {
  o <- tempfile(fileext = ".png"); image_write(image_trim(image_read_svg(P(f), width = 900), fuzz = 8), o); o
}, character(1))
imgH <- function(p, h) sprintf("<img src='%s' height='%d'/>", p, h)
imgW <- function(p, w) sprintf("<img src='%s' width='%d'/>", p, w)
sym  <- function(ch, pt) sprintf("<span style='font-size:%dpt'>%s</span>", pt, ch)

# pMCMC = twice the smaller posterior tail probability of the paired difference.
pmcmc <- function(d) { n <- length(d); max(2 * min(sum(d > 0) / n, sum(d < 0) / n), 1 / n) }
fmtp  <- function(p) ifelse(p < 0.001, "p<0.001", paste0("p=", sub("^0", "", sprintf("%.3f", p))))
make_brackets <- function(case, glevels) {
  ylev <- rev(glevels); ypos <- setNames(seq_along(ylev), ylev); prs <- combn(glevels, 2, simplify = FALSE)
  dc <- filter(fig4_draws, case_study == case); rows <- list()
  for (q in as.character(unique(dc$quantity))) {
    dq <- filter(dc, quantity == q); rng <- range(dq$value); w <- diff(rng); tick <- 0.04 * w
    sp <- sapply(prs, function(pr) abs(ypos[[pr[1]]] - ypos[[pr[2]]])); maxsp <- max(sp)
    for (i in seq_along(prs)) {
      pr <- prs[[i]]; ya <- ypos[[pr[1]]]; yb <- ypos[[pr[2]]]; span <- sp[i]
      xbar <- rng[2] + w * (0.06 + 0.16 * (span - 1))
      # paired by .draw (joint zebrafish fit; matched draws for the per-sex fly fits)
      m <- inner_join(filter(dq, group == pr[1])[, c(".draw", "value")],
                      filter(dq, group == pr[2])[, c(".draw", "value")],
                      by = ".draw", suffix = c("_a", "_b"))
      p <- pmcmc(m$value_a - m$value_b); side <- if (span == maxsp) "R" else "L"
      rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- tibble(quantity = factor(q, levels = levels(fig4_draws$quantity)),
        x = xbar, xt = xbar - tick, side = side,
        xtext = if (side == "R") xbar + 0.03 * w else xbar - tick - 0.02 * w,
        y = min(ya, yb), yend = max(ya, yb), label = fmtp(p))
    }
  }
  bind_rows(rows)
}
add_brackets <- function(p, case, glevels) {
  br <- make_brackets(case, glevels)
  p + geom_segment(data = br, aes(x = x, xend = x, y = y, yend = yend), inherit.aes = FALSE, linewidth = .45, colour = "grey30") +
      geom_segment(data = br, aes(x = xt, xend = x, y = y, yend = y), inherit.aes = FALSE, linewidth = .45, colour = "grey30") +
      geom_segment(data = br, aes(x = xt, xend = x, y = yend, yend = yend), inherit.aes = FALSE, linewidth = .45, colour = "grey30") +
      geom_text(data = filter(br, side == "L"), aes(x = xtext, y = (y + yend) / 2, label = label), inherit.aes = FALSE, hjust = 1, size = 3.1, colour = "grey10", fontface = "bold") +
      geom_text(data = filter(br, side == "R"), aes(x = xtext, y = (y + yend) / 2, label = label), inherit.aes = FALSE, hjust = 0, size = 3.1, colour = "grey10", fontface = "bold")
}
fig4_panel <- function(cs, glevels, ylabels, title, sci, tag,
                       texp = 1.15, xexp_r = 0.05, ridge_scale = 0.65,
                       ridge_scale_ctmax = ridge_scale) {
  d <- filter(fig4_draws,   case_study == cs) |> mutate(group = factor(group, levels = rev(glevels)))
  s <- filter(fig4_summary, case_study == cs) |> mutate(group = factor(group, levels = rev(glevels)), ci_col = fig4_pal_dark[as.character(group)])
  ggplot(d, aes(value, group, fill = group)) +
    geom_density_ridges(
      data = dplyr::filter(d, !as.character(quantity) %in% c("CTmax_1hr", "CTmax_4hr")),
      colour = "white", linewidth = .2, alpha = .9, scale = ridge_scale,
      rel_min_height = .005
    ) +
    geom_density_ridges(
      data = dplyr::filter(d, as.character(quantity) %in% c("CTmax_1hr", "CTmax_4hr")),
      colour = "white", linewidth = .2, alpha = .9, scale = ridge_scale_ctmax,
      rel_min_height = .005
    ) +
    geom_linerange(data = s, aes(y = group, xmin = lower, xmax = upper, colour = ci_col), inherit.aes = FALSE, linewidth = 1.3) +
    geom_point(data = s, aes(median, group, fill = group), inherit.aes = FALSE, shape = 21, colour = "black", size = 3.6, stroke = 0.7) +
    geom_label(data = s, aes(median, group, label = sprintf("%.1f [%.1f, %.1f]", median, lower, upper)), inherit.aes = FALSE, vjust = -0.7, size = 3.2, fontface = "bold", colour = "grey10", fill = alpha("white", 0.7), label.size = 0, label.padding = unit(0.08, "lines")) +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x", nrow = 1, strip.position = "bottom", labeller = labeller(quantity = qlab)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = fig4_pal, guide = "none") + scale_colour_identity() +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(.05, xexp_r))) +
    scale_y_discrete(labels = ylabels, expand = expansion(mult = c(.06, texp))) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = title, subtitle = sci, tag = tag) +
    theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
    theme(strip.background = element_blank(), strip.placement = "outside", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
          axis.text.y = element_markdown(), plot.tag = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15), plot.subtitle = element_text(face = "italic", size = 11.5, colour = "grey30"),
          panel.border = element_rect(colour = "grey75", fill = NA, linewidth = .4), plot.margin = margin(4, 4, 4, 4))
}
ins <- function(p, f, l, b, r, t) p + inset_element(ggdraw() + draw_image(f), left = l, bottom = b, right = r, top = t, align_to = "full")

fig4_A <- ins(fig4_panel("Brown shrimp", "Brown shrimp", c("Brown shrimp" = ""), "Brown shrimp", "Crangon crangon", "a)", ridge_scale_ctmax = .28), svg_png("shrimp_v2.svg"), .23, .58, .62, .93)
fig4_B <- ins(fig4_panel("Snow gum leaf PSII", "Snow gum leaf PSII", c("Snow gum leaf PSII" = ""), "Snow gum", "Eucalyptus pauciflora", "b)"), svg_png("snowgum_v4.svg"), .06, .60, .46, .88)
fig4_C <- add_brackets(fig4_panel("Zebrafish", zf_stages,
            setNames(c(imgH(danio_trim["young_embryos"], 50), imgH(danio_trim["old_embryos"], 50), imgW(danio_trim["larvae"], 62)), zf_stages),
            "Zebrafish", "Danio rerio", "c)", texp = 0.62, xexp_r = 0.32), "Zebrafish", zf_stages)
fig4_C <- ins(fig4_C, svg_png("zebrafish_v2.svg"), .27, .62, .57, .90)
fig4_D <- add_brackets(fig4_panel("Vinegar fly (mortality)", c("Female", "Male"), c(Female = sym("♀", 30), Male = sym("♂", 30)), "Vinegar fly", "Drosophila suzukii", "d)", texp = 1.35, xexp_r = 0.27), "Vinegar fly (mortality)", c("Female", "Male"))
fig4_D <- ins(fig4_D, svg_png("dros_male_v2.svg"), .18, .62, .48, .82)

fig4_final <- (fig4_A | fig4_B) / (fig4_C | fig4_D)

# Also write a standalone high-res PNG so the assembled figure can be imported
# directly (e.g. into the manuscript or slides) without re-running the chunk.
dir.create(here("output", "figs"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
ggsave(here("output", "figs", "fig4_case_study_densities.png"), fig4_final,
       width = 11.5, height = 9.6, dpi = 600, bg = "white")

# Display the saved PNG rather than the live plot object, so the figure's tuned
# canvas (11.5 x 9.6 in) and inset/bracket positions are not re-flowed by the
# chunk's fig dimensions (mirrors the ms.qmd include_graphics approach).
knitr::include_graphics(here("output", "figs", "fig4_case_study_densities.png"))
```

# **Manuscript Figure 6: Heat injury and survival** {#sec-ms-fig5}

Manuscript Figure 6 illustrates how a fitted joint 4PL translates a *natural*
temperature trajectory into cumulative heat-injury (HI) accumulation and
predicted survival, with uncertainty, for two case studies side by side: the
vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) and the brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*).
For the fly we use the shaded hourly **microclimate** trace from the
Ørsted et al. (2024) NicheMapR workflow (Rennes, France; June-August 2018;
Zenodo record 10821572) and predict HI for each sex with and without damage
repair.

```{r}
#| label: shrimp-trace-facts
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# Code-generated facts for the shrimp heat-injury trace, so the text, caption
# and data README never drift from the shipped data or the fitted T_crit.
shr_year   <- 2021L   # hottest fjord summer by peak hourly SST across 2008-2024
shr_offset <- 3       # warming projection (deg C) added to the observed trace
shr_Tc     <- et_shrimp$T_crit$summary$temp_median   # corrected relative-threshold T_crit
.kb_facts  <- utils::read.csv(here::here("inst", "extdata", "kristineberg",
                                         "kristineberg_sea_temp_hourly.csv.gz"))
.kb_facts  <- .kb_facts[is.finite(.kb_facts$sea_temp_c), ]
.summer    <- .kb_facts[substr(.kb_facts$datetime_utc, 6, 7) %in% c("06", "07", "08"), ]
shr_max_file  <- max(.kb_facts$sea_temp_c)                          # whole-record hourly maximum
shr_peak_year <- max(.summer$sea_temp_c[.summer$year == shr_year])  # chosen summer's peak hour
shr_n_years   <- length(unique(.kb_facts$year))
```

For the shrimp we downloaded the full hourly
**sea-surface**-temperature record (2008–2024) from the Kristineberg Marine
Research Station on the Gullmar fjord, Sweden — a coastal site within the brown
shrimp's range — from the station's public weather archive
(`www.weather.mi.gu.se/kristineberg`). We use June–August of `r shr_year`, the
warmest summer on record by peak hourly temperature (peaking at
`r round(shr_peak_year, 1)` °C). Even this warmest summer only just reaches this
shrimp population's corrected $T_{crit}$ (`r round(shr_Tc, 1)` °C), and across the
whole `r shr_n_years`-year record the hourly maximum is only
`r round(shr_max_file, 1)` °C, so a literal trace accumulates little or no
injury. We therefore added a **+`r shr_offset` °C warming projection** — keeping
the real diurnal and seasonal structure but shifting it warm enough to cross
$T_{crit}$ — and label it as a scenario, not observed lethal exposure. Repair is
modelled with an **illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield kernel that is not
empirically fitted** — it shows the *qualitative* effect of repair, not a
calibrated rate. We set $r_{ref}=0.03$ LT50-dose h$^{-1}$ at the reference
temperature, so a full LT50 dose would take roughly 33 h to clear at that
temperature in the absence of further injury. Bands are ±1 SE across posterior draws (not 95% credible
intervals) to keep the exponential HI envelope readable. $T_{crit}$ is the
relative-threshold critical temperature from each fit.

```{r}
#| label: fig5-hi-data
#| cache: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Prepare temperature traces and posterior summaries for the manuscript heat-injury figure.
## Fly: shaded hourly microclimate temperature from the Oersted et al. (2024)
## Zenodo/NicheMapR workflow (Rennes, France; Jun-Aug 2018). The cached CSV is
## built by data-raw/build_orsted_microclimate_trace.R from the original
## microclimate_injury_accumulation.R script in Zenodo record 10821572.
orsted_micro <- utils::read.csv(here::here(
  "inst", "extdata", "orsted_2024",
  "orsted2024_nichemapr_rennes_2018_hourly.csv.gz"
))
fly_trace <- orsted_micro |>
  dplyr::mutate(datetime_utc = as.POSIXct(datetime_utc, tz = "UTC")) |>
  dplyr::filter(substr(datetime_utc, 1, 4) == "2018",
                substr(datetime_utc, 6, 7) %in% c("06", "07", "08"),
                is.finite(micro_temp_c)) |>
  dplyr::arrange(datetime_utc) |>
  dplyr::transmute(time = dplyr::row_number() - 1, temp = micro_temp_c,
                   datetime = datetime_utc)

## Relative-threshold T_crit per group (matches the relative HI integration).
Tc_F <- extract_tdt(wf_dros_mort_F, target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60,
                    lethal = TRUE, ndraws = 150)$T_crit$summary$temp_median
Tc_M <- extract_tdt(wf_dros_mort_M, target_surv = "relative", t_ref = 60,
                    lethal = TRUE, ndraws = 150)$T_crit$summary$temp_median

## Illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield repair kernel (doses h^-1 at TREF; NOT fitted).
fly_repair <- list(TA = 14065, TAL = 50000, TAH = 100000,
                   TL = 15 + 273.15, TH = 30 + 273.15,
                   TREF = 25 + 273.15, r_ref = 0.03)

ND <- 150
agg_draws <- function(hi, ...) {
  hi$draws |> dplyr::group_by(time) |>
    dplyr::summarise(hi_med = median(hi), hi_se = sd(hi),
                     surv_med = median(survival), surv_se = sd(survival),
                     .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::mutate(...)
}

fly_rows <- list()
for (sx in c("Female", "Male")) {
  wf <- if (sx == "Female") wf_dros_mort_F else wf_dros_mort_M
  Tc <- if (sx == "Female") Tc_F else Tc_M
  for (rep in c(FALSE, TRUE)) {
    hi <- predict_heat_injury(fly_trace[, c("time", "temp")], wf,
            target_surv = "relative", T_c = Tc, trace_unit = "hours",
            ndraws = ND, repair = rep,
            repair_pars = if (rep) fly_repair else NULL, save_draws = TRUE)
    fly_rows[[length(fly_rows) + 1]] <-
      agg_draws(hi, sex = sx, repair = ifelse(rep, "With repair", "No repair"))
  }
}
fig5_fly <- dplyr::bind_rows(fly_rows) |> dplyr::left_join(fly_trace, by = "time")

## Shrimp: REAL Kristineberg (Gullmar fjord) hourly sea-surface temperature for
## the warmest summer on record by peak hourly temperature (shr_year; Jun-Aug),
## warmed by a +shr_offset C projection. The observed fjord trace only just
## reaches T_crit, so the modest offset is needed to show heat-injury dynamics
## and is labelled as a projection in the caption. Year / offset / T_crit come
## from the code-generated `shrimp-trace-facts` chunk above. See
## data-raw/build_kristineberg_temps.R and inst/extdata/kristineberg/README.md.
Tc_S <- shr_Tc
kb <- utils::read.csv(here::here("inst", "extdata", "kristineberg",
                                 "kristineberg_sea_temp_hourly.csv.gz"))
shr_trace <- kb |>
  dplyr::filter(year == shr_year, substr(datetime_utc, 6, 7) %in% c("06", "07", "08"),
                is.finite(sea_temp_c)) |>
  dplyr::arrange(datetime_utc) |>
  dplyr::transmute(time = dplyr::row_number() - 1,
                   temp = sea_temp_c + shr_offset,
                   datetime = as.POSIXct(datetime_utc, tz = "UTC"))
shr_repair <- list(TA = 14065, TAL = 50000, TAH = 100000,
                   TL = 8 + 273.15, TH = 22 + 273.15,
                   TREF = 15 + 273.15, r_ref = 0.03)
shr_rows <- list()
for (rep in c(FALSE, TRUE)) {
  hi <- predict_heat_injury(shr_trace[, c("time", "temp")], wf_shrimp,
          target_surv = "relative", T_c = Tc_S, trace_unit = "hours",
          ndraws = ND, repair = rep,
          repair_pars = if (rep) shr_repair else NULL, save_draws = TRUE)
  shr_rows[[length(shr_rows) + 1]] <-
    agg_draws(hi, repair = ifelse(rep, "With repair", "No repair"))
}
fig5_shr <- dplyr::bind_rows(shr_rows) |> dplyr::left_join(shr_trace, by = "time")

dir.create(here::here("output", "data"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
saveRDS(list(fly = fig5_fly, fly_trace = fly_trace, fly_Tc = mean(c(Tc_F, Tc_M)),
             shr = fig5_shr, shr_trace = shr_trace, shr_Tc = Tc_S,
             shr_year = shr_year, shr_offset = shr_offset,
             shr_max_file = shr_max_file, shr_peak_year = shr_peak_year,
             shr_n_years = shr_n_years),
        here::here("output", "data", "fig5_hi_data.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-ms-fig5-heat-injury
#| fig-cap: "Manuscript Figure 6. Heat-injury accumulation and predicted survival under field-derived temperature trajectories for the vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*, panels a-c) and the brown shrimp (*Crangon crangon*, panels d-f). (a, d) the temperature trace — shaded NicheMapR microclimate temperature for the fly (Rennes, France, Jun–Aug 2018; Ørsted et al. 2024 Zenodo record 10821572) and real Gullmar fjord summer sea-surface temperature for the shrimp (Kristineberg Marine Research Station, Jun–Aug 2021, the warmest summer on record by peak temperature) warmed by a **+3 °C projection** — with the relative-threshold $T_{crit}$ (purple dotted line). The observed fjord trace only just reaches the shrimp's $T_{crit}$, so the modest warming projection makes the heat-injury dynamics visible. (b, e) cumulative heat injury (100% = one $LT_{50}$ dose, dashed) and (c, f) predicted survival, each with and without damage repair (colour); fly sexes are distinguished by line type. Bands are ±1 SE across posterior draws. Predicted survival begins at each taxon's fitted upper asymptote rather than at 100%: the vinegar-fly 4PL estimates a maximum survival of ≈85% (the upper asymptote *up* < 1), so its survival trajectories start at ≈85%, whereas the shrimp asymptote is ≈100%. Repair uses an illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield kernel with $r_{ref}=0.03$ LT50-dose h$^{-1}$ at the reference temperature; it is not empirically fitted."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
#| cache: false

# Build the manuscript heat-injury and survival prediction figure.
f5  <- readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig5_hi_data.rds"))
fdat <- f5$fly; ftr <- f5$fly_trace; fTc <- f5$fly_Tc
sdat <- f5$shr; str <- f5$shr_trace; sTc <- f5$shr_Tc
fdat$repair <- factor(fdat$repair, levels = c("No repair", "With repair"))
sdat$repair <- factor(sdat$repair, levels = c("No repair", "With repair"))
pal <- c("No repair" = "#D55E00", "With repair" = "#0072B2")
tcrit_col <- "#7B3294"

f5_thm <- theme_classic(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey93"),
        plot.margin = margin(2, 7, 2, 3), legend.position = "none",
        axis.title = element_text(size = 14), axis.text = element_text(size = 12))
f5_xsc <- function() scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "1 month", date_labels = "%b")
f5_tg  <- function(p, lab) p + labs(tag = lab) +
  theme(plot.tag = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12), plot.tag.position = c(0.015, 0.98))

f5_temp <- function(tr, Tc, title, ylab) ggplot(tr, aes(datetime, temp)) +
  geom_line(colour = "grey45", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = Tc, linetype = "dotted", colour = tcrit_col) +
  annotate("text", x = min(tr$datetime), y = Tc, label = "T[crit]",
           hjust = -0.1, vjust = -0.4, size = 3.4, colour = tcrit_col,
           parse = TRUE) +
  labs(y = ylab, x = NULL, title = title) + f5_xsc() + f5_thm +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_markdown(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5))

f5_hi <- function(dd, lt) {
  g <- ggplot(dd, aes(datetime, hi_med, colour = repair, fill = repair)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = pmax(0, hi_med - hi_se), ymax = hi_med + hi_se),
                colour = NA, alpha = 0.15) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
    scale_colour_manual(values = pal) + scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
    labs(y = "Heat injury (%)", x = NULL) + f5_xsc() + f5_thm +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_blank())
  if (lt) g + geom_line(aes(linetype = sex), linewidth = 0.55) +
            scale_linetype_manual(values = c(Female = "solid", Male = "22"))
  else    g + geom_line(linewidth = 0.6)
}
f5_surv <- function(dd, lt) {
  g <- ggplot(dd, aes(datetime, surv_med, colour = repair, fill = repair)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = pmax(0, surv_med - surv_se), ymax = pmin(1, surv_med + surv_se)),
                colour = NA, alpha = 0.15) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = pal) + scale_fill_manual(values = pal) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent) +
    labs(y = "Survival", x = NULL) + f5_xsc() + f5_thm
  if (lt) g + geom_line(aes(linetype = sex), linewidth = 0.55) +
            scale_linetype_manual(values = c(Female = "solid", Male = "22"))
  else    g + geom_line(linewidth = 0.6)
}
f5_ins <- function(p, f, l, b, r, t, sc = 1) p +
  inset_element(ggdraw() + draw_image(svg_png(f), scale = sc),
                left = l, bottom = b, right = r, top = t, align_to = "panel")

fly_hi <- f5_ins(f5_tg(f5_hi(fdat, TRUE),  "b)"), "dros_male_v2.svg", .02, .55, .34, .98)
shr_hi <- f5_ins(f5_tg(f5_hi(sdat, FALSE), "e)"), "shrimp_v2.svg",    .04, .58, .44, .96)
f5_leg <- cowplot::get_legend(
  f5_hi(fdat, TRUE) + theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_blank()) +
    guides(fill = "none", colour = guide_legend(order = 1), linetype = guide_legend(order = 2)))

col_f <- f5_tg(f5_temp(ftr, fTc, "Vinegar fly (*D. suzukii*)",  "Microclimate temp (°C)"), "a)") / fly_hi / f5_tg(f5_surv(fdat, TRUE),  "c)")
col_s <- f5_tg(f5_temp(str, sTc, "Brown shrimp (*C. crangon*)", "Water temp (°C)"), "d)") / shr_hi / f5_tg(f5_surv(sdat, FALSE), "f)")
f5_body  <- (col_f | col_s) + plot_layout(heights = c(0.5, 1, 1))
fig5_final <- cowplot::plot_grid(f5_body, f5_leg, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(1, 0.06))

ggsave(here::here("output", "figs", "fig5_heat_injury.png"), fig5_final,
       width = 9.5, height = 7.8, dpi = 600, bg = "white")

# Display the saved PNG (not the live object) so the tuned 9.5 x 7.8 in canvas
# and inset positions are not re-flowed by the chunk's fig dimensions.
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig5_heat_injury.png"))
```


# **Derivation of the correction factors for threshold summaries**

The manuscript states two correction factors without showing the algebra: an
asymmetry correction *added* to $\text{mid}(T)$ to recover
$\log_{10}t_{50}(T)$ from a 4PL whose asymptotes are not 0 and 1 (Eq. (4)),
and the corresponding correction *subtracted* from the naive
$CT_{max}(t_{\text{ref}})$ (Eq. (5)). Here we derive both expressions, extend
them to an arbitrary survival threshold $x/100$, and check them against direct
4PL inversion.

## Why a correction is needed {#sec-supp-corr-why}

The 4PL midpoint is the $\log_{10}$-time value at which survival reaches the
average of the two asymptotes, $(\text{up}+\text{low})/2$. When
$\text{low}\approx0$ and $\text{up}\approx1$, this point is also near 50%
survival. With partial endpoints, however, the midpoint need not occur at
$p=0.5$: if $\text{up}=0.85$ and $\text{low}=0.05$, it occurs at 0.45. The
absolute $\log_{10}t_{50}$ therefore comes from inverting the 4PL at $p=0.5$.
The signed offset between $\text{mid}(T)$ and this absolute crossing is the
**asymmetry correction**.

## Step-by-step derivation of the $\log_{10}t_{50}$ correction {#sec-supp-corr-lt50}

Starting from Eq. (2) of the manuscript with the temperature-dependent midpoint
from Eq. (3), the survival probability is
$$
p(t, T) \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t - \text{mid}(T))\bigr)}.
$$

By definition $\log_{10}t_{50}(T)$ is the value of $\log_{10}t$ at which
$p = 0.5$. Solving for it gives:

**Step 1 — set $p = 0.5$:**
$$
0.5 \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr)}.
$$

**Step 2 — move $\text{low}$ to the left:**
$$
0.5 - \text{low} \;=\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr)}.
$$

**Step 3 — invert both sides:**
$$
\frac{1}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;=\; \frac{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr)}{\text{up} - \text{low}}.
$$

**Step 4 — multiply both sides by $(\text{up} - \text{low})$:**
$$
\frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;=\; 1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr).
$$

**Step 5 — subtract 1 to isolate the exponential:**
$$
\frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;-\; 1 \;=\; \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr).
$$

The left-hand side simplifies:
$$
\frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{0.5 - \text{low}} - 1 \;=\; \frac{(\text{up} - \text{low}) - (0.5 - \text{low})}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;=\; \frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$

Hence
$$
\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;=\; \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T))\bigr).
$$

**Step 6 — take natural logs of both sides:**
$$
\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;=\; k\,\bigl(\log_{10}t_{50}(T) - \text{mid}(T)\bigr).
$$

**Step 7 — divide by $k$ and add $\text{mid}(T)$:**
$$
\log_{10}t_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-supp-corr-lt50}

Thus $\log_{10}t_{50}$ is the midpoint at that temperature plus a correction
that depends only on $(\text{low}, \text{up})$ and $k$, not on $T$. This is Eq.
(4) of the manuscript.

**Step 8 — substitute the temperature-dependent midpoint** from Eq. (3) of the
manuscript:
$$
\log_{10}t_{50}(T) \;=\; \bigl[\beta_0 + \beta_1(T - \bar T)\bigr] \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$

**Step 9 — distribute and group all $T$-independent terms into an intercept
$\alpha$:**
$$
\boxed{\quad\log_{10}t_{50}(T) \;=\; \alpha + \beta_1 T, \qquad \alpha \;=\; \beta_0 - \beta_1 \bar T \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.\quad}
$$ {#eq-supp-tdt-line}

This recovers the classical TDT relation: $\log_{10}t_{50}$ is linear in $T$.

## From $\log_{10}t_{50}$ to the $CT_{max}$ correction {#sec-supp-corr-ctmax}

By definition, $CT_{max}(t_{\text{ref}})$ is the temperature at which
$t_{50}=t_{\text{ref}}$, or
$\log_{10}t_{50}(T)=\log_{10}t_{\text{ref}}$. From @eq-supp-tdt-line:
$$
\log_{10}t_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \alpha \;+\; \beta_1\,CT_{max}(t_{\text{ref}})
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
CT_{max}(t_{\text{ref}}) \;=\; \frac{\log_{10}t_{\text{ref}} - \alpha}{\beta_1}.
$$

Substituting the full $\alpha$ from @eq-supp-tdt-line and tidying:
$$
CT_{max}(t_{\text{ref}}) \;=\; \bar T \;+\; \frac{\log_{10}t_{\text{ref}} - \beta_0}{\beta_1} \;-\; \frac{1}{\beta_1}\cdot\frac{1}{k}\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-supp-corr-ctmax}

The first two terms are the *naive* $CT_{max}$: the temperature at which
$\beta_0 + \beta_1(T-\bar T)$ crosses $\log_{10}t_{\text{ref}}$. The final term
is the asymmetry correction divided by $\beta_1$, appearing as a subtraction
because a vertical shift on the log-time axis becomes a horizontal temperature
shift when divided by the TDT slope. This is Eq. (5) of the manuscript.

## Generalisation to arbitrary survival thresholds $x/100$ {#sec-supp-corr-ltx}

Replacing $p = 0.5$ with $p = x/100$ gives
$$
\log_{10}t_x(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\log\!\frac{\text{up} - x/100}{x/100 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-supp-corr-ltx}
which reduces to @eq-supp-corr-lt50 when $x = 50$. The correction is still
added to $\text{mid}(T)$; its sign is set by the ratio inside the log. For
$x < 50$ (e.g. LT10), the correction is positive and LT$_x$ occurs later than
the midpoint. For $x > 50$ (e.g. LT90), it is negative and LT$_x$ occurs
earlier. The corresponding $CT_{max,x}(t_{\text{ref}})$ follows by substituting
this LT$_x$ correction into @eq-supp-corr-ctmax.

## The slope (and hence $z$) is robust to the correction {#sec-supp-corr-z-robust}

Because the correction in @eq-supp-corr-lt50 does not depend on $T$, it shifts
$\log_{10}t_{50}(T)$ vertically without changing the slope:
$$
z \;=\; -\frac{1}{\beta_1}
$$
holds provided the 4PL is correctly specified. This is why the joint Bayesian
4PL and the classical two-stage pipeline can recover the same $z$ even when
their $CT_{max}$ estimates differ. $CT_{max}$ has no analogous protection: the
same asymmetry term enters @eq-supp-corr-ctmax divided by $\beta_1$, so errors
in $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, or $k$ shift the temperature estimate directly.

## When the correction vanishes {#sec-supp-corr-vanish}

When the experimental design anchors $\text{low} \approx 0$ and $\text{up}
\approx 1$:
$$
\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;\approx\; \frac{1 - 0.5}{0.5 - 0} \;=\; 1
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\log\!\frac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}} \;\approx\; \log 1 \;=\; 0.
$$
The correction then shrinks to zero and
$\text{mid}(T)\approx\log_{10}t_{50}(T)$. This is the equivalence regime in
@sec-twostage and @sec-joint, and the strict-equivalence baseline of the
simulation in §3.1. The correction matters when the design is partial:
sublethal endpoints, sparse survival-range coverage, or asymptotes that vary
with $T$ (the last case is analysed in @sec-joint-extend).

## Numerical sanity check {#sec-supp-corr-numerical}

The chunk below uses asymmetric asymptotes so the correction is non-zero. It
checks that @eq-supp-corr-lt50 matches direct 4PL inversion at $p = 0.5$, and
that @eq-supp-corr-ctmax matches the temperature obtained by direct inversion
at $t_{\text{ref}}=60$ min.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-supp-corr-check
#| tbl-cap: "Verification that the closed-form $\\log_{10}t_{50}$ correction (@eq-supp-corr-lt50) agrees with direct numerical inversion of the 4PL at $p = 0.5$, and that $CT_{max,1h}$ from @eq-supp-corr-ctmax agrees with direct inversion at $t = 60$ min."
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Check the one-step correction against numerical inversion.
low_a  <- 0.05
up_a   <- 0.85           # asymmetric: (up - 0.5) != (0.5 - low), so the correction is non-zero
kk     <- 4
beta_0 <- log10(60)      # midpoint at T_bar sits exactly at log10(60 min)
beta_1 <- -0.25          # so z = -1/beta_1 = 4 °C
T_bar  <- 36

correction <- (1 / kk) * log((up_a - 0.5) / (0.5 - low_a))

# Closed form (eq-supp-corr-lt50) at T = 38 °C.
T_test <- 38
mid_T  <- beta_0 + beta_1 * (T_test - T_bar)
log10_LT50_closed <- mid_T + correction

# Direct numerical inversion: find log10 t such that p(t, T) = 0.5.
p_4pl <- function(log10_t, T_val) {
  midT <- beta_0 + beta_1 * (T_val - T_bar)
  low_a + (up_a - low_a) / (1 + exp(kk * (log10_t - midT)))
}
log10_LT50_direct <- stats::uniroot(
  function(x) p_4pl(x, T_test) - 0.5,
  interval = c(-5, 10)
)$root

# Closed-form CTmax_1hr from eq-supp-corr-ctmax. Because mid(T) was written
# with centred temperature, beta_0 + beta_1 * (T - T_bar), alpha converts the
# line to the uncentred form alpha + beta_1 * T before inversion.
alpha             <- beta_0 - beta_1 * T_bar + correction
CT_max_1hr_closed <- (log10(60) - alpha) / beta_1
z_from_b1         <- -1 / beta_1

# Direct cross-check for CTmax_1hr: find T such that the 4PL crosses p = 0.5
# exactly at t = 60 min.
CT_max_1hr_direct <- stats::uniroot(
  function(T_val) p_4pl(log10(60), T_val) - 0.5,
  interval = c(20, 50)
)$root

data.frame(
  Quantity = c("Asymmetry correction (1/k) log[(up-0.5)/(0.5-low)]",
               "log10(LT50) at T = 38 °C, closed form (eq-supp-corr-lt50)",
               "log10(LT50) at T = 38 °C, direct 4PL inversion",
               "  |closed - direct|",
               "z derived from beta_1 (expected: 4)",
               "CTmax_1hr, closed form (eq-supp-corr-ctmax)",
               "CTmax_1hr, direct 4PL inversion at t = 60 min",
               "  |closed - direct|"),
  Value    = c(sprintf("%+.6f", correction),
               sprintf("%.6f", log10_LT50_closed),
               sprintf("%.6f", log10_LT50_direct),
               sprintf("%.3e", abs(log10_LT50_closed - log10_LT50_direct)),
               sprintf("%.4f °C", z_from_b1),
               sprintf("%.4f °C", CT_max_1hr_closed),
               sprintf("%.4f °C", CT_max_1hr_direct),
               sprintf("%.3e", abs(CT_max_1hr_closed - CT_max_1hr_direct)))
) |>
  tinytable::tt(escape = TRUE)
```

Here the correction is **non-zero** (`{r} sprintf("%+.4f", correction)`), and
the closed forms match direct inversion to floating-point precision. The slope
still gives $z=-1/\beta_1=4$ °C. In contrast, $CT_{max,1h}$ is shifted from
$\bar T=36$ °C by about $-\text{correction}/\beta_1$, showing why the
correction matters when $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ are not equidistant from
0.5.

# **Session Information**

```{r session-info}
#| label: session-info
#| results: asis

# Record package versions and session details for reproducibility.
pander::pander(sessionInfo())
```
